The divergence was consistent across multiple analytical approaches, and in some instances agents explicitly acknowledged in their private channel that public accommodation was driven by relational pressures such as career risk or sponsorship obligations. The authors conclude that standard agent evaluation focused on explicit goals is insufficient, and propose that dual-channel frameworks and behavioral measures are needed to detect latent objectives that emerge from social context.
items.citation: Arman Ghaffarizadeh, Danyal Mohaddes, Aliakbar Izadkhah et al.. What LLM Agents Say When No One Is Watching: Social Structure and Latent Objective Emergence in Multi-Agent Debates. arXiv. 2026

items.title: EAGLE-360: Embodied Active Global-to-Local Exploration in 360$^\circ$
items.summary: This paper investigates the challenge of adapting Multimodal Large Language Models for active visual search within 360° panoramic environments, where standard models fail due to difficulties handling polar distortion and continuous cylindrical topologies. The authors propose EAGLE-360, a framework that uses global panoramic priors to establish an initial holistic perspective before iteratively narrowing the search space, rather than relying on fragmented local viewpoints. To handle panoramic geometry, they adapt a coordinate-shifting positional encoding mechanism called RoPE Rolling, and they construct a large-scale dataset of high-resolution panoramas paired with extensive visual question-answering dialogues to support training. The system is trained using a combination of supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning via Group Relative Policy Optimization to elicit spatial reasoning and tool-calling behaviors. Experiments show that EAGLE-360 achieves a new state-of-the-art for 360° visual search with a dramatic improvement in accuracy over the base model alongside substantially greater exploration efficiency.
items.citation: Jingtao Xu, Zizhuo Lin, Jianwen Sun et al.. EAGLE-360: Embodied Active Global-to-Local Exploration in 360$^\circ$. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Cryogenic RF characterization of the MAGO cavity for high-frequency gravitational-wave detection
items.summary: This paper investigated the use of superconducting radio-frequency cavities as resonant sensors for detecting gravitational waves in the kilohertz-to-megahertz frequency range. The researchers characterized a prototype superconducting niobium cavity featuring an unconventional geometry designed to achieve narrow separation between electromagnetic modes, applying adapted surface preparation procedures before conducting cryogenic tests at temperatures down to two kelvin. The measurements confirmed high electromagnetic quality factors consistent with prior prototype results and achieved a very small mode splitting through mechanical tuning, while also revealing phase transfer properties relevant for stable low-level RF control. The study additionally uncovered indications of mode coupling possibly arising from one-point multipacting and found that mechanical quality factors were significantly lower than commonly assumed theoretical values. Overall, the work demonstrates that established superconducting radio-frequency preparation and characterization techniques can be successfully applied to non-standard resonator geometries, providing key experimental guidance for future gravitational-wave detector development.
items.citation: Can Dokuyucu, Bianca Giaccone, Tom Krokotsch et al.. Cryogenic RF characterization of the MAGO cavity for high-frequency gravitational-wave detection. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Interpretation-Oriented Cloud Removal via Observation-Anchored Residual Flow with Geo-Contextual Alignment
items.summary: This paper addresses the problem of cloud removal in optical remote sensing imagery, specifically examining how existing methods prioritize visual quality at the expense of semantic accuracy needed for downstream tasks like segmentation and change detection. The authors propose a unified framework called Geo-Anchored Cloud Removal that treats cloud removal as a physically grounded residual inversion process, anchoring the generative trajectory to the cloudy observation itself rather than pure noise to enable more stable and faithful reconstruction. The framework also incorporates a Geo-Contextual Prior Alignment module that leverages a Vision Foundation Model to constrain reconstructions within a semantic manifold, thereby preserving spatial-semantic integrity across complex landscapes. Evaluated across multiple cloud removal datasets and a broad range of downstream tasks, the proposed method demonstrates improvements in both reconstruction quality and downstream task performance compared to existing approaches.
items.citation: Ziyao Wang, Maonan Wang, Yucheng He et al.. Interpretation-Oriented Cloud Removal via Observation-Anchored Residual Flow with Geo-Contextual Alignment. arXiv. 2026

items.title: AgentsCAD: Automated Design for Manufacturing of FDM Parts via Multi-Agent LLM Reasoning and Geometric Feature Recognition
items.summary: This paper investigated the challenge of automating Design for Additive Manufacturing modifications for parts produced via Fused Deposition Modeling, where current slicing software can detect geometric defects like steep overhangs but cannot alter the underlying geometry to fix them. The researchers developed AgentsCAD, a multi-agent system that parses standard STEP file geometry into a boundary-representation format, detects overhangs exceeding a threshold angle, builds a face-adjacency topology graph, and optionally enriches the representation with semantic feature labels from a graph neural network trained on a large dataset of mechanical parts. A large language model reasoning agent then recommends geometric modifications such as reorientations, fillets, and chamfers, while a separate vision-language model verifies the geometric integrity of the resulting design through rendered views. Testing on a representative model demonstrated that the system correctly identifies overhang defects, selects appropriate mitigation strategies, and proposes physically valid corrections. The authors conclude that their approach partially addresses the core problem of translating geometric representations into a form amenable to language-model-driven CAD modification.
items.citation: Emmanuel George, Christopher Keefe, Peter Pak et al.. AgentsCAD: Automated Design for Manufacturing of FDM Parts via Multi-Agent LLM Reasoning and Geometric Feature Recognition. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Rapid and Sensitive Detection of <i>Schistosoma mansoni</i> in the Intermediate Snail Hosts Using Loop-Mediated Isothermal Amplification (LAMP) Diagnostics.
items.summary: The paper investigated improved diagnostic methods for detecting *Schistosoma mansoni* infections in freshwater snails, which serve as intermediate hosts for the parasite causing schistosomiasis. Researchers developed a loop-mediated isothermal amplification assay targeting a mitochondrial gene of the parasite, optimized for a simple one-hour reaction at a fixed temperature, and evaluated its performance against conventional microscopy, standard PCR, and real-time PCR using both laboratory-infected snails and field samples from two endemic countries. The new assay achieved complete specificity and high sensitivity in laboratory settings, outperforming microscopy and performing comparably to real-time PCR. In field evaluations, it detected a higher proportion of infected snails than conventional PCR, while microscopy failed to identify any positive snails at all. The authors conclude that this assay represents a sensitive, specific, and practically deployable tool suitable for routine surveillance of snail infection and early detection of changes in schistosomiasis transmission.
items.citation: Li HM, Qin ZQ, Lv S et al.. Rapid and Sensitive Detection of <i>Schistosoma mansoni</i> in the Intermediate Snail Hosts Using Loop-Mediated Isothermal Amplification (LAMP) Diagnostics.. Tropical medicine and infectious disease. 2026

items.title: You Only Look Once (YOLO) based machine learning algorithm for real-time detection of loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) diagnostics.
items.summary: The paper investigated whether a machine learning approach could automate the interpretation of loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) diagnostic results, which are typically read visually and therefore subject to human error. The researchers applied the YOLOv8 object detection algorithm to digital images of LAMP reaction tubes, training it to locate individual tubes and classify them as positive or negative for amplification. The model was evaluated on a test set and achieved high overall accuracy, with strong precision and recall for both positive and negative classifications. The findings suggest that this approach can reliably reduce subjectivity in LAMP result interpretation and is suitable for real-time diagnostic deployment with straightforward operation and rapid output.
items.citation: Mezgebo B, Chaffee R, Castellanos LR et al.. You Only Look Once (YOLO) based machine learning algorithm for real-time detection of loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) diagnostics.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Recent advances in isothermal amplification techniques for detection of animal diseases.
items.summary: This review examines isothermal amplification technologies as diagnostic tools for detecting pathogens in livestock and companion animals. The authors address limitations of conventional and PCR-based molecular methods, which typically require costly equipment, dedicated laboratory infrastructure, and specialized expertise that restrict their use in field or resource-limited settings. The review synthesizes recent advances in isothermal amplification approaches, covering improvements in assay design, analytical performance, and integration into portable, field-deployable detection platforms. The authors find that these technologies offer rapid, sensitive, and user-friendly nucleic acid detection without thermocycling equipment, making them strong alternatives for veterinary diagnostics. The review also outlines remaining challenges and future directions for incorporating isothermal methods into routine veterinary diagnostics and broader animal disease surveillance programs.
items.citation: Tooryan F, Enyertornye B, Choudhury FJ et al.. Recent advances in isothermal amplification techniques for detection of animal diseases.. Frontiers in veterinary science. 2026

items.title: Development of a digital loop-mediated isothermal amplification using a digital polymerase chain reaction device.
items.summary: This study developed a digital loop-mediated isothermal amplification workflow by adapting a commercially available digital polymerase chain reaction platform, addressing limitations of conventional isothermal methods such as variability and limited partition counts. The researchers optimized reactions targeting a mitochondrial genetic marker from a freshwater mussel species using a diluted fluorescent dye master mix on a high-density nanoplate system. The resulting method achieved absolute quantification across a broad dynamic range without requiring standard curves, and demonstrated stable, reliable detection down to very low copy numbers. Compared to conventional turbidimetric bulk assays, which showed moderate variability and occasional delayed amplification at low concentrations, the digital approach provided enhanced quantitative stability. The authors propose this platform as a practical tool for applications including diagnostics, food safety, and environmental DNA monitoring where accurate low-copy-number detection is critical.
items.venue: Analytical sciences : the international journal of the Japan Society for Analytical Chemistry
items.citation: Fukuta S, Suzuki R. Development of a digital loop-mediated isothermal amplification using a digital polymerase chain reaction device.. Analytical sciences : the international journal of the Japan Society for Analytical Chemistry. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examines the landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for rapid, decentralized testing outside conventional laboratory settings. The authors organized their analysis around the three core operational steps of such diagnostics: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. They surveyed extraction strategies including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic approaches, alongside amplification methods such as PCR, NASBA, RPA, and LAMP, all of which have been adapted for portable and low-power conditions. For detection, they reviewed platforms ranging from colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays to advanced biosensors exploiting CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch principles. The review concludes that current point-of-care nucleic acid platforms span a broad range of technologies capable of delivering sensitive, selective, and economically viable infectious disease testing in decentralized environments.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

items.title: Numerical study of the ITER divertor plasma with the B2-EIRENE code package
items.summary: The abstract is too thin to summarize, as it contains only bibliographic and repository information with no scientific content about the study's methods, results, or findings.
items.citation: V. Kotov. Numerical study of the ITER divertor plasma with the B2-EIRENE code package. Dokumentenrepositorium der RUB (Ruhr University Bochum). 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly known as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their growing impact on laboratory medicine and diagnostics. The authors surveyed innovations in chip design and fabrication methods, including three-dimensional printing and flexible substrates, alongside the integration of advanced biosensors capable of detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes in real time. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has produced smart diagnostic platforms offering automated analysis, self-optimization, and clinical decision support for applications ranging from cancer diagnostics to point-of-care testing. The review also explores emerging uses in neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling, illustrating the broad clinical potential of these technologies. The authors additionally address outstanding challenges in manufacturing, clinical validation, and system integration that must be resolved before next-generation microfluidic devices can be routinely deployed in clinical and public health settings.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Biosensing technologies for foodborne pathogen detection and healthcare: principles, emerging materials, and intelligent platforms
items.summary: This review examines the current state and future directions of biosensing platforms designed to detect high-priority foodborne pathogens, including Escherichia coli, Salmonella, and Listeria monocytogenes, with relevance to both food safety and clinical diagnostics. The authors survey electrochemical, optical, and microfluidic sensing modalities, emphasizing how advances in functional nanomaterials, aptamers, nanozymes, and surface engineering have improved sensor sensitivity, specificity, and performance in complex food matrices. A central theme is the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning into these platforms, enabling real-time analytics, intelligent pattern recognition, and high-throughput multiplexed detection that transforms conventional sensors into smart diagnostic devices. The review critically addresses persistent challenges such as matrix interference, bioreceptor instability, manufacturing scalability, and the absence of regulatory standardization, proposing mitigation strategies including ratiometric sensing, microfluidic pre-treatment, and explainable AI frameworks. Looking ahead, the authors advocate for self-powered biosensors, modular pathogen detection panels, blockchain-based traceability, and standardized validation pipelines as pathways toward regulation-ready, next-generation food safety monitoring systems.
items.citation: Purshottam J. Assudani, Balakrishnan . P., Anny Leema A et al.. Biosensing technologies for foodborne pathogen detection and healthcare: principles, emerging materials, and intelligent platforms. Microchimica Acta. 2026

items.title: SARS-CoV-2 wastewater genomic surveillance: approaches, challenges, and opportunities
items.summary: Wastewater-based genomic surveillance has emerged as a valuable tool for tracking SARS-CoV-2 variants and other viruses at the community level, offering the ability to detect known and emerging mutations without relying solely on clinical testing. This review examines the full pipeline of wastewater genomic surveillance, encompassing sample preparation, sequencing technologies, and bioinformatics methods used to characterize circulating viral lineages. The authors identify key challenges in the field, particularly the difficulty of distinguishing between closely related lineages and identifying genuinely novel ones from complex environmental samples. They note that many recently developed analytical methods remain inadequately tested in the context of reduced clinical surveillance and continuous viral evolution. The review concludes by highlighting the potential of wastewater genomic surveillance to serve as a robust component of public health monitoring systems, while calling attention to the technical and computational limitations that must be addressed.
items.citation: Viorel Munteanu, Michael A. Saldana, David Dreifuss et al.. SARS-CoV-2 wastewater genomic surveillance: approaches, challenges, and opportunities. Genome biology. 2026

items.title: Early diagnosis and treatment of leptospirosis: Optimizing clinical outcomes
items.summary: This review paper examined the challenge of late diagnosis in leptospirosis, a widespread zoonotic disease responsible for substantial global mortality that is frequently identified only after serious organ complications have developed. The authors analyzed the limitations of existing clinical and laboratory diagnostic approaches alongside emerging molecular and biomarker-based tools, as well as integrated scoring systems for triage. A central focus was the therapeutic window during the leptospiremic phase, when antibiotic treatment is most effective but is often missed due to delayed recognition. The review synthesized evidence on early antibiotic therapy, supportive care strategies, and severity prediction tools. Based on this analysis, the authors advocate for a paradigm shift toward point-of-care, field-adaptable diagnostics and integrated care pathways to enable earlier intervention and reduce the disease's global burden.
items.citation: Umaporn Limothai, Nattachai Srisawat, David A. Haake. Early diagnosis and treatment of leptospirosis: Optimizing clinical outcomes. Journal of Infection. 2026

items.title: Sensitizing effect of lanthanide luminescence by Mo4+/Ag+ in double perovskites: great enhancement of near-infrared emission via wide range of excitation (250–850 nm)
items.summary: This study investigated lead-free halide double perovskites doped with lanthanide ions to address their limitations in near-infrared applications, specifically their narrow emission bands and low photoluminescence quantum yields caused by weak light absorption. The researchers synthesized a cesium sodium indium chloride host material and co-doped it with molybdenum and silver ions alongside various lanthanide dopants to overcome these shortcomings. The co-doping strategy achieved near-unity photoluminescence quantum yield and dramatically expanded the usable excitation range to span from ultraviolet through visible light and into the near-infrared region. Mechanistic investigation revealed that synergistic energy transfer through self-trapping excitons and intermediate energy states of the molybdenum ion together enhanced photon harvesting and emission efficiency. The resulting materials demonstrated broad near-infrared emission suitable for light-emitting diodes, night vision, bioimaging, and anti-counterfeiting applications, and the approach was validated across multiple lanthanide dopants and bismuth-based host systems, establishing it as a general strategy for improving lanthanide-activated optoelectronic materials.
items.citation: Yingsheng Wang, Peipei Dang, Zixun Zeng et al.. Sensitizing effect of lanthanide luminescence by Mo4+/Ag+ in double perovskites: great enhancement of near-infrared emission via wide range of excitation (250–850 nm). Light Science & Applications. 2026

items.title: Topological Control of Quantum Chaos Diagnostics: OTOCs, Spectral Statistics, and Information Scrambling in Ising Model
items.summary: This paper investigates how the topology of spin interaction networks governs the transition from integrable to chaotic quantum dynamics and the scrambling of quantum information. The authors model Ising spin systems using a graph-theoretic framework, representing spins as vertices and interactions through adjacency matrices for three distinct network topologies: path graphs, Erdős–Rényi random networks, and Watts–Strogatz small-world networks. By tuning non-local coupling strengths and field heterogeneity, they drive integrability breaking and characterize the resulting chaos through multiple complementary diagnostics, including out-of-time-order correlators, Krylov complexity, bipartite and tripartite mutual information, and spectral statistics. They find that long-range couplings and heterogeneous degree distributions dramatically accelerate quantum information propagation, with tripartite information reaching large negative values and OTOCs displaying exponential early-time growth characteristic of chaotic dynamics, while level spacing statistics shift from Poissonian to Wigner–Dyson distributions. The work establishes a unified framework linking network topology to information-theoretic, operator-growth, and spectral chaos diagnostics, demonstrating that a reduced Thouless time strongly correlates with faster scrambling across all measures.
items.citation: Reza Pirmoradian, Soheir Rouhani, M. Reza Tanhayi. Topological Control of Quantum Chaos Diagnostics: OTOCs, Spectral Statistics, and Information Scrambling in Ising Model. arXiv. 2026

items.title: EvoPolicyGym: Evaluating Autonomous Policy Evolution in Interactive Environments
items.summary: This paper introduces a framework for evaluating how well autonomous agents can iteratively improve executable policies through feedback in controlled environments. The authors define Autonomous Policy Evolution as a setting where an agent repeatedly edits a policy system within a fixed interaction budget, rather than being assessed on a single final outcome or open-ended software engineering tasks. They instantiate this framework in EvoPolicyGym, a benchmark built from compact interactive reinforcement learning environments designed to measure iterative policy improvement. Testing a range of models on this suite, one language model achieved the strongest overall performance across all evaluated environments. Beyond ranking models, the benchmark provides trajectory-level diagnostics revealing that effective autonomous policy evolution requires not just winning individual tasks but discovering task-appropriate mechanisms and efficiently converting feedback into policy refinements under bounded interaction budgets.
items.citation: Zhilin Wang, Han Song, Runzhe Zhan et al.. EvoPolicyGym: Evaluating Autonomous Policy Evolution in Interactive Environments. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Native-Opacity Sensitivity of a Fixed Delta Cephei MESA-RSP Pulsation Model
items.summary: This paper investigated how the choice of radiative opacity tables affects the pulsation behavior of a classical Cepheid variable star model, specifically using the well-studied delta Cephei system as a benchmark. The researchers held all stellar and pulsation parameters fixed in a nonlinear radial pulsation model computed with the MESA Radial Stellar Pulsation module, varying only the high-temperature opacity source among three native MESA configurations to isolate the effect of opacity table selection. They found that the choice of opacity table measurably influences period matching with observations, pulsation growth diagnostics, and nonlinear amplitude growth, with one configuration producing a period within seconds of the observed value while another yielded substantially larger amplitude-growth diagnostics. The three opacity sources produced a consistent ordering across multiple diagnostic metrics, demonstrating that opacity selection is not a negligible modeling choice even when all other parameters are held constant. However, none of the tested opacity configurations alone resolved the known discrepancy between modeled and observed pulsation amplitudes, indicating that opacity selection is an important but insufficient lever for achieving full observational agreement.
items.citation: Zuhoor Elahi, Christopher Sirola, Wafa Gull. Native-Opacity Sensitivity of a Fixed Delta Cephei MESA-RSP Pulsation Model. arXiv. 2026

items.title: MARVEL: Margin-Aware Robust von Mises-Fischer Expert Learning for Long-Tailed Out-of-Distribution Detection
items.summary: The paper addresses the challenge of out-of-distribution detection in automated medical diagnostic systems, where deep learning models tend to incorrectly assign high confidence to inputs they have never encountered before. The authors developed a framework called MARVEL, which combines a Nonlinear von Mises-Fisher classifier capable of learning non-linear decision boundaries with a multi-expert system in which margin-aware classifiers specialize across different regions of the label distribution to handle class imbalance, alongside a dedicated outlier expert trained to distinguish familiar from unfamiliar inputs. The approach was specifically designed for imbalanced medical datasets and evaluated across clinically realistic out-of-distribution scenarios using retinal, skin lesion, and colorectal tissue datasets. Across all three benchmarks, MARVEL consistently outperformed existing state-of-the-art methods, achieving substantial reductions in the false positive rate at high true positive sensitivity. The results support the use of this framework for safely deferring uncertain cases to clinicians in real-world AI-assisted diagnostic workflows.
items.citation: A. S. Anudeep, Vaanathi Sundaresan. MARVEL: Margin-Aware Robust von Mises-Fischer Expert Learning for Long-Tailed Out-of-Distribution Detection. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Self-Auditing Residual Drifting for Pathology-Preserving Accelerated Knee MRI
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of accelerated knee MRI reconstruction, where undersampling k-space can obscure diagnostically important structures in ways that standard image quality metrics fail to detect. The authors propose a method called SA-RDM-DC, which adapts a generative drifting framework to MRI by training a physics-conditioned model to predict residual corrections from zero-filled reconstructions toward fully sampled images, incorporating data consistency, frequency-aware supervision, and simultaneous generation of error maps and per-slice risk scores within a single inference pass. The approach was evaluated on multi-coil knee MRI datasets at multiple acceleration factors, with pathology annotations enabling assessment of lesion preservation alongside conventional image quality measures. Compared to several baselines including diffusion-based and variational network approaches, the proposed method achieved superior structural similarity while maintaining fast inference and avoiding the lengthy sampling times typical of iterative diffusion models. The self-auditing mechanism successfully identified high-error reconstructions and showed partial transferability as a quality-screening signal under protocol shift, supporting an evaluation framework that jointly considers image fidelity, pathology preservation, computational efficiency, and case-specific reliability.
items.citation: Qing Lyu, Jianxu Wang, Mohammad Kawas et al.. Self-Auditing Residual Drifting for Pathology-Preserving Accelerated Knee MRI. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Time-Reversal and Reversible Dynamics in Cavity QED for Quantum Metrology
items.summary: This review paper examines how time-reversal and reversible many-body dynamics in cavity quantum electrodynamics can be harnessed to improve quantum-enhanced metrology. The central challenge addressed is that while highly entangled states can surpass the standard quantum limit in sensitivity, extracting their metrological advantage requires measurement strategies capable of decoding the encoded information. The authors review how many-body interactions can serve a dual purpose—both generating entanglement and decoding it—through interaction-based readout and time-reversal protocols such as signal amplification through a time-reversed interaction and scrambling-enhanced metrology. Cavity QED is highlighted as a particularly suitable platform because it offers collective enhancement, tunable interactions, and controllable reversibility within a unified system. The review concludes that the capacity to decode quantum information through reversible dynamics may prove as important as generating entanglement itself, positioning reversible many-body dynamics as a fundamental resource for quantum-enhanced sensing.
items.citation: Simone Colombo, Edwin Pedrozo-Peñafiel. Time-Reversal and Reversible Dynamics in Cavity QED for Quantum Metrology. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic value of cystatin C in acute kidney injury among patients with sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
items.citation: Cheng L, Zhang B, Hu X et al.. Diagnostic value of cystatin C in acute kidney injury among patients with sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Frontiers in medicine. 2026

items.title: Pancreatic Stone Protein as an Emerging Biomarker in Paediatric Nosocomial and Postoperative Sepsis: An Integrative Review of Diagnostic and Prognostic Performance.
items.summary: This narrative review examined the diagnostic and prognostic utility of pancreatic stone protein as a biomarker for sepsis in paediatric patients, with particular focus on nosocomial and postoperative settings where distinguishing infectious from sterile inflammation is clinically difficult. The authors conducted a structured literature search across two major databases, screening studies published over a recent multi-year period and ultimately including over fifty studies meeting their criteria. The review found that pancreatic stone protein levels rise significantly in children with sepsis compared to those with non-infectious systemic inflammation, and elevated levels were associated with bacteraemia, organ dysfunction, and mortality. Diagnostic performance was reported to be comparable to or superior to conventional markers such as C-reactive protein and procalcitonin, with particularly notable sensitivity and specificity in neonatal and other high-risk paediatric populations. The authors conclude that pancreatic stone protein is a promising early biomarker for paediatric sepsis, while noting that study heterogeneity necessitates larger prospective multicentre trials and integration into multimarker diagnostic frameworks before clinical adoption.
items.citation: Ramírez Quintana A, Gaman EL, Badea M et al.. Pancreatic Stone Protein as an Emerging Biomarker in Paediatric Nosocomial and Postoperative Sepsis: An Integrative Review of Diagnostic and Prognostic Performance.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: Early diagnostic accuracy of immature platelet fraction for sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
items.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis investigated whether the immature platelet fraction, a measure of bone marrow thrombopoietic activity, could serve as an early diagnostic biomarker for sepsis. The authors searched two major databases and identified studies meeting inclusion criteria, then pooled data to calculate sensitivity, specificity, and diagnostic odds ratios, including a subgroup analysis focused on adult populations. The meta-analysis found that the immature platelet fraction demonstrated only moderate diagnostic accuracy for sepsis, with pooled sensitivity and specificity both in the low-to-mid seventies percent range and moderate heterogeneity across the included studies. The authors conclude that while the immature platelet fraction may have complementary value as a sepsis biomarker, the evidence base is very limited and methodologically heterogeneous, warranting cautious interpretation. They call for further standardized prospective studies before its routine clinical application can be recommended.
items.citation: Wiranata S, Manuaba IBAP, Supadmanaba IGP et al.. Early diagnostic accuracy of immature platelet fraction for sepsis: a systematic review and meta-analysis.. Hematology, transfusion and cell therapy. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic performance of interleukin-27 and C-reactive protein in neonatal sepsis: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis.
items.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated and compared the diagnostic accuracy of interleukin-27 and C-reactive protein as biomarkers for neonatal sepsis, a condition where existing evidence had been inconsistent. Following PRISMA guidelines, the authors searched multiple major databases spanning nearly three decades and pooled data from controlled studies involving nearly five hundred neonates, calculating sensitivity, specificity, and area under the summary receiver operating characteristic curve for both biomarkers. The analysis found that interleukin-27 demonstrated higher pooled sensitivity and specificity than C-reactive protein, and its area under the curve was notably superior. The authors conclude that interleukin-27 has greater diagnostic accuracy than C-reactive protein for identifying neonatal sepsis and may serve as a more reliable biomarker to support early and accurate clinical diagnosis.
items.citation: Liu W, Zhang Y, Liu W et al.. Diagnostic performance of interleukin-27 and C-reactive protein in neonatal sepsis: an updated systematic review and meta-analysis.. Frontiers in pediatrics. 2026

items.title: Aligning neonatal sepsis triage diagnostics with WHO target product profiles in low- and middle-income countries.
items.summary: This paper examined the state of diagnostics for neonatal sepsis in low- and middle-income countries, where the condition remains a major driver of infant death and illness despite limited progress in diagnostic development. The authors synthesized evidence across a broad range of current and emerging approaches, including clinical algorithms, host-response biomarkers, haematological parameters, continuous monitoring, and artificial intelligence-based digital platforms, evaluating each against the WHO Target Product Profile for neonatal sepsis triage. The analysis assessed not only diagnostic performance but also operational feasibility and affordability in resource-constrained and non-hospital settings. The review identified several promising technologies, including host-response biomarkers with greater accuracy than those currently in use and affordable tools capable of enhancing existing predictive algorithms. The authors conclude that integrated diagnostic strategies combining point-of-care testing, routine clinical data, and digital decision support represent the most viable path toward earlier detection, more rational antibiotic use, and reduced neonatal mortality in these settings.
items.citation: Gleeson B, Blumel B, Ibarz Pavon AB et al.. Aligning neonatal sepsis triage diagnostics with WHO target product profiles in low- and middle-income countries.. BMJ global health. 2026

items.title: Calprotectin as a Diagnostic Marker for Lower Respiratory Tract Infection and Sepsis in the Emergency Department.
items.summary: This study investigated whether calprotectin, a neutrophil activation marker measured in plasma, could serve as a useful diagnostic biomarker for identifying sepsis and distinguishing its source of infection in emergency department patients. Researchers prospectively enrolled several hundred patients presenting with suspected sepsis to a university hospital emergency department and measured calprotectin levels at admission, comparing its performance against established markers such as C-reactive protein. Calprotectin showed modest ability to discriminate between sepsis and non-infectious systemic inflammatory response syndrome, but was not an independent predictor when C-reactive protein was included in multivariable analysis. However, calprotectin demonstrated meaningful ability to differentiate lower respiratory tract infections from both upper respiratory tract infections and urinary tract infections, and adding calprotectin to C-reactive protein significantly improved discriminatory performance for these comparisons. The authors conclude that calprotectin may complement existing markers in identifying the source of infection in sepsis patients, while calling for further research to clarify its added value in routine clinical assessment.
items.citation: Wollmer M, Wessman T, Melander O et al.. Calprotectin as a Diagnostic Marker for Lower Respiratory Tract Infection and Sepsis in the Emergency Department.. Open forum infectious diseases. 2026

items.title: GLP-1 receptor agonists and next-generation incretin-based medications: metabolic, cardiovascular, and renal benefits
items.summary: This review examines the therapeutic profile of glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists and next-generation incretin-based medicines across metabolic, cardiovascular, and renal disease. Originally developed for type 2 diabetes, these agents provide effective glycaemic control with weight reduction and low hypoglycaemia risk, while also reducing major adverse cardiovascular events, hospitalisation for heart failure, albuminuria, and the rate of kidney function decline. Approved agents and the dual GIP/GLP-1 co-agonist tirzepatide have demonstrated additional benefits in obesity management, including prevention of type 2 diabetes, improvement in heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, regression of hepatic steatosis, and symptomatic relief in obstructive sleep apnoea and knee osteoarthritis. Emerging research is exploring potential utility in neurodegenerative diseases and substance use disorders, alongside development of oral small-molecule formulations and multi-receptor agonists targeting combinations of GLP-1, GIP, glucagon, and amylin receptors to achieve greater efficacy, particularly for weight loss. Gastrointestinal adverse effects remain a notable tolerability challenge, and optimisation of dose-escalation strategies is identified as an ongoing priority.
items.citation: Michael A Nauck, KATHERINE R. TUTTLE, Matthias H. Tschöp et al.. GLP-1 receptor agonists and next-generation incretin-based medications: metabolic, cardiovascular, and renal benefits. The Lancet. 2026

items.title: Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation
items.summary: This review examines the expanding role of neutrophils in chronic inflammatory diseases, moving beyond their classical function as short-lived innate immune cells that rapidly clear pathogens. The authors synthesize current understanding of how neutrophils adopt a hyperinflammatory phenotype in chronic disease contexts, characterized by dysregulated mechanisms including excessive NET formation, uncontrolled oxidative burst, aberrant degranulation, and sustained release of proteases and cytokines that drive tissue damage. The review also highlights how neutrophils actively communicate with other immune and non-immune cell types—including endothelial cells, monocytes, platelets, and lymphocytes—to orchestrate broader inflammatory and tissue-remodeling processes. These neutrophil-driven mechanisms are discussed in relation to specific immune-mediated conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, atherosclerosis, inflammatory bowel disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and fibrotic disorders. The authors propose that deepening insight into neutrophil behavior during chronic inflammation may illuminate disease pathology and reveal novel therapeutic targets.
items.citation: Kaat Torfs, Gaël Vermeersch, Mieke Gouwy et al.. Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation. Cellular and Molecular Immunology. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and how its disruption contributes to a broad range of diseases. The authors synthesize the research history of lipid metabolism and emphasize its importance in immune cell biology, covering how macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils depend on lipid metabolites for proliferation, differentiation, and activation across both innate and adaptive immunity. The review addresses lipid transport, signaling pathways, and multilevel regulatory mechanisms spanning genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational processes that govern immune cell function. The authors further discuss how dysregulated lipid metabolism underlies conditions including autoimmune diseases, cancer, neurodegeneration, cardiovascular disease, aging, and metabolic disorders. The review concludes by surveying therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, ongoing clinical efforts, and emerging directions such as lipid-derived biomarkers and novel treatment approaches.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the relationship between the microbiota and cancer, encompassing carcinogenesis, tumor progression, prognosis, and treatment response. The authors trace the historical development of microbiota-cancer research and synthesize current knowledge on the molecular mechanisms by which microbial communities influence oncogenic signaling pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual nature of the microbiota as both a promoter and inhibitor of tumor growth, and explores how microbial communities modulate responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Therapeutic strategies targeting the microbiota—including probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—are discussed alongside approved drugs and ongoing clinical trials. The authors conclude by advocating for personalized, microbiota-targeted oncological approaches and propose a comprehensive framework to guide future research and clinical applications in this field.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Pathophysiology of reactive oxygen species (ROS)
items.summary: This review paper examines the pathophysiology of reactive oxygen species, synthesizing a decade of advances in understanding how these molecules act as either beneficial signaling mediators or damaging agents depending on context. The authors describe how outcomes are governed by the specific generating source, subcellular location, and kinetics of production—a framework they term the "where–when–how much" rule—with low, compartmentalized flux enabling reversible cysteine-based signaling while excess production drives oxidative damage, lipid peroxidation, and regulated cell death including ferroptosis. The review integrates these mechanisms with disease processes spanning cardiovascular, metabolic, neurodegenerative, oncologic, pulmonary, and renal conditions, emphasizing crosstalk with reactive nitrogen and sulfur species as well as iron metabolism. On the methodological side, the authors advocate for species-resolved, compartment-aware measurement tools rather than generic oxidative stress assays. Therapeutically, they argue against broad antioxidant supplementation in favor of targeted, node-specific interventions delivered in time-staged sequences and guided by biomarkers, reframing reactive oxygen species as precision redox modulators rather than generic toxins.
authors: José Manuel Pérez de la Lastra
items.citation: José Manuel Pérez de la Lastra, Celia María Curieses Andrés, Elena Bustamante Munguira et al.. Pathophysiology of reactive oxygen species (ROS). Archives of Toxicology. 2026

items.title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
items.summary: This paper presents the 2026 Surviving Sepsis Campaign international guidelines for the management of sepsis and septic shock in adults. The guidelines were developed by a large, geographically diverse multidisciplinary committee using the GRADE methodology, incorporating structured evidence synthesis, patient and family advisory input, and an Evidence to Decision framework to formulate recommendations across six clinical domains including screening, infection management, hemodynamics, respiratory support, adjunctive therapies, and goals of care. The guidelines emphasize early identification and treatment, antimicrobial stewardship through responsible use and de-escalation, and the importance of standardized sepsis screening and treatment pathways in both hospital and prehospital settings. Recommendations range from strong to conditional depending on the certainty of evidence and balance of effects, with explicit attention to applicability in low- and middle-income country contexts. The guidelines aim to translate a broad body of research into actionable clinical guidance to improve outcomes for the millions of patients affected by sepsis annually.
items.citation: Hallie C. Prescott, Massimo Antonelli, Waleed Alhazzani et al.. Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026. Critical Care Medicine. 2026

items.title: Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs
items.summary: The paper investigates methods for monitoring large language model outputs in real time to detect unsafe or incorrect generations during deployment, even after alignment training. The approach involves using an external model as a verifier that produces a signal, which is then converted into an alarm by applying a threshold calibrated through risk control techniques. The study evaluates this simple thresholding monitor against more sophisticated approaches based on sequential hypothesis testing, using mathematical reasoning and red-teaming datasets as test cases. The central finding is that despite its simplicity, the threshold-based monitor performs comparably to the more complex sequential testing alternatives, suggesting that straightforward calibrated thresholding is a viable strategy for online safety monitoring of language models.
items.citation: Mona Schirmer, Metod Jazbec, Alexander Timans et al.. Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation
items.summary: This paper addresses a fundamental problem in on-policy self-distillation for training large language models to reason, where a single model serves as both teacher and student with different levels of information access. The authors identify two core failure modes of existing approaches: overfitting to in-domain patterns due to dense token-level supervision, and privileged information leakage, where the student learns shortcuts tied to information unavailable at test time. To resolve these issues, they introduce DemoPSD, a framework that selectively adopts teacher guidance by steering the student toward a reverse-KL barycenter target—a weighted geometric combination of teacher and student distributions—where the blending at each token position is adaptively controlled by the measured divergence between the two distributions. Theoretically, the authors prove that DemoPSD simultaneously attenuates privileged information leakage and preserves exploration capacity during training. Empirical evaluation across multiple scientific domains demonstrates that DemoPSD outperforms existing baselines while maintaining higher training entropy and generalizing more robustly to out-of-distribution benchmarks.
items.citation: Yunhe Li, Hao Shi, Wenhao Liu et al.. DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of building traffic simulation agents that both realistically imitate recorded driving behavior and can be deliberately steered along interpretable behavioral dimensions. The authors developed Controllable Neural Variational Agents, a framework that infers per-agent behavior latents from discounted returns using a closed-form variational update, and combines this with a rectified-flow trajectory generator trained with classifier-free guidance via a mixed channel-mask curriculum. To address sparse reward signals, they introduced soft eligibility gates that replace hard binary thresholds with smooth exponential decay, preserving gradient information for agents near reward boundaries. Evaluated on a large-scale motion dataset, the framework achieves competitive realism while enabling monotone, interpretable control over speed, acceleration, safety, and map compliance in ways that purely imitation-based models cannot offer. The work also highlights that steering metrics must be interpreted alongside physical plausibility checks to avoid reward-hacking artifacts that can otherwise obscure genuine behavioral control.
items.citation: Juanwu Lu, Junyu Zhu, Ziran Wang. Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Cryogenic RF characterization of the MAGO cavity for high-frequency gravitational-wave detection
items.summary: This paper studied the radio-frequency and mechanical properties of a superconducting niobium cavity with an unconventional geometry intended for detecting gravitational waves in the kilohertz-to-megahertz frequency range. The researchers applied adapted surface preparation procedures to the prototype cavity and conducted cryogenic tests at temperatures down to two kelvin at two different facilities. They found that mechanical tuning achieved a very narrow electromagnetic mode splitting at cryogenic temperature, and high electromagnetic quality factors consistent with earlier prototypes were measured. The measurements also revealed phase transfer characteristics relevant for stable control systems, indications of mode coupling possibly linked to one-point multipacting, and mechanical quality factors significantly lower than commonly assumed theoretical values. Overall, the results demonstrate that established superconducting radio-frequency techniques can be successfully applied to non-standard resonator geometries, providing key experimental inputs for future gravitational-wave detector development.
items.citation: Can Dokuyucu, Bianca Giaccone, Tom Krokotsch et al.. Cryogenic RF characterization of the MAGO cavity for high-frequency gravitational-wave detection. arXiv. 2026

items.title: TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution
items.summary: This paper addresses a gap in evaluating test automation systems by introducing a benchmark specifically designed for test and code co-evolution tasks, where changes to source code should be accompanied by corresponding updates or additions to tests. The authors mined real commit histories from open-source Java projects to construct tasks in two tracks: generating new tests to capture changed software behavior, and updating existing failing tests to reflect code modifications. A key design feature is that the benchmark is executable, meaning tasks are verified through actual runtime metrics such as pass rate, code coverage, and mutation score, rather than static metadata alone. The benchmark is also "live," with automated periodic mining of new tasks timestamped to allow evaluation restricted to data postdating a model's training cutoff, thereby reducing data leakage. Experiments with state-of-the-art agents combining strong scaffolding frameworks with leading foundation models show promising but imperfect performance, with success rates dropping notably on the most recent tasks and under constrained computational budgets, suggesting current systems do not fully generalize to novel co-evolution scenarios.
items.citation: Jiale Amber Wang, Kaiyuan Wang, Pengyu Nie. TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Topological Control of Quantum Chaos Diagnostics: OTOCs, Spectral Statistics, and Information Scrambling in Ising Model
items.summary: This paper investigates how the topology of spin network connectivity governs the transition from integrable to chaotic quantum dynamics in Ising models. The authors formulated the problem graph-theoretically, representing spins as vertices and interactions through adjacency matrices for path, Erdős–Rényi, and Watts–Strogatz network topologies, with a Hamiltonian that allows tuning of non-local coupling strength and field heterogeneity. Using a suite of diagnostics—including out-of-time-order correlators, Krylov complexity, bipartite and tripartite mutual information, level spacing statistics, and the spectral form factor—they characterized how network structure shapes quantum chaos and information scrambling. They found that long-range couplings and heterogeneous degree distributions dramatically accelerate operator growth and information propagation, driving tripartite information to large negative values indicative of deep scrambling, and shifting level statistics from Poissonian to Wigner–Dyson distributions. A key finding is that a reduced Thouless time, extracted from the spectral form factor, correlates strongly with accelerated scrambling, establishing a unified connection between network topology and thermalization dynamics in quantum many-body systems.
items.citation: Reza Pirmoradian, Soheir Rouhani, M. Reza Tanhayi. Topological Control of Quantum Chaos Diagnostics: OTOCs, Spectral Statistics, and Information Scrambling in Ising Model. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Rapid Detection of Pathogens and Resistance Genes in Positive Blood Cultures Using BioFire<sup>®</sup> FilmArray BCID2.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a multiplex molecular panel for identifying pathogens and antimicrobial resistance genes directly from positive blood cultures. Researchers compared the panel's results against conventional culture-based methods, mass spectrometry identification, and automated susceptibility testing in samples collected from a university hospital, with resistance gene findings further confirmed by polymerase chain reaction. The panel successfully detected at least one microorganism in the large majority of samples, demonstrating strong agreement with culture-based identification across key species, including complete concordance for certain gram-negative pathogens, and resistance gene detection correlated well with phenotypic susceptibility results. The panel also identified a substantial proportion of samples as polymicrobial, though interpretation was acknowledged to be limited by the absence of clinical correlation data and information on repeat cultures, particularly for coagulase-negative staphylococci. The authors conclude that the panel provides rapid and reliable pathogen and resistance gene identification, making it a valuable complement to conventional diagnostics when results are interpreted in clinical context.
items.citation: Çinar C, Çayci YT, Atakcan Z et al.. Rapid Detection of Pathogens and Resistance Genes in Positive Blood Cultures Using BioFire<sup>®</sup> FilmArray BCID2.. Polish journal of microbiology. 2026

items.title: When Faster Diagnosis Is Not Enough: Bringing Diagnostic Stewardship into Antimicrobial Stewardship.
items.citation: Mezzadri L, Bonfanti P. When Faster Diagnosis Is Not Enough: Bringing Diagnostic Stewardship into Antimicrobial Stewardship.. Le infezioni in medicina. 2026

items.title: Multiplex mismatch amplification mutation polymerase chain reaction assay for the rapid detection of antimicrobial-resistant determinants in Cutibacterium acnes.
items.summary: This study addressed the growing clinical problem of antimicrobial resistance in Cutibacterium acnes, a bacterium central to the pathogenesis of acne vulgaris. Researchers developed a multiplex mismatch amplification mutation assay PCR capable of simultaneously detecting multiple resistance determinants, including point mutations in ribosomal RNA genes and a DNA gyrase gene, as well as acquired resistance genes associated with macrolide and tetracycline resistance. The assay was validated against a large collection of clinical isolates characterized by conventional antimicrobial susceptibility testing and genetic analysis. The optimized protocol completed detection in approximately one hour and achieved perfect sensitivity and specificity relative to conventional methods. The findings support this multiplex PCR approach as a rapid and accurate diagnostic tool to guide appropriate antimicrobial therapy for acne and to help monitor the spread of resistant strains.
items.citation: Yoshihara K, Seyama S, Hayashi N et al.. Multiplex mismatch amplification mutation polymerase chain reaction assay for the rapid detection of antimicrobial-resistant determinants in Cutibacterium acnes.. Diagnostic microbiology and infectious disease. 2026

items.title: A review of bloodstream infections-pathogens, pathogenesis, diagnostic strategies, treatment methods-challenges and future aspects.
items.summary: This review examines bloodstream infections, a major global cause of illness and death in which pathogenic microorganisms enter the circulatory system and can trigger severe systemic responses including sepsis, septic shock, and multi-organ failure. The authors conducted a comprehensive synthesis of peer-reviewed literature, clinical guidelines, and surveillance reports spanning over a decade, covering bacterial, fungal, viral, and parasitic pathogens alongside their virulence mechanisms, immune evasion strategies, and resistance profiles. The review finds that bacterial pathogens predominate as causative agents while Candida species are the leading fungal contributors, with increasing antimicrobial resistance and diagnostic delays continuing to drive substantial healthcare burden. Emerging technologies including molecular diagnostics, metagenomic sequencing, biomarker-guided testing, and artificial intelligence-assisted analyses have meaningfully advanced rapid pathogen detection and clinical decision-making. The authors conclude that integrating next-generation diagnostics, genomic surveillance, and precision medicine approaches alongside robust antimicrobial stewardship holds considerable promise for improving individualized management of these complex infections.
items.venue: European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology
items.citation: Kumar A, Kumar A, Tyagi A et al.. A review of bloodstream infections-pathogens, pathogenesis, diagnostic strategies, treatment methods-challenges and future aspects.. European journal of clinical microbiology & infectious diseases : official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology. 2026

items.title: Graphene foam biosensors for molecular diagnostics of antimicrobial resistance and emerging pathogen threats.
items.summary: This paper developed an electrochemical biosensor platform designed for rapid, point-of-care detection of antimicrobial resistance genes and emerging pathogen targets. The platform uses disposable graphene foam electrodes functionalized with pyrene-modified peptide nucleic acid probes, which are immobilized through π-π stacking in a process that avoids chemical surface reactions and preserves the electrode's electronic properties. A key innovation is a heat-assisted functionalization protocol that reduces preparation time from hours to minutes without sacrificing sensor performance. The biosensor achieved amplification- and label-free detection of the colistin resistance gene and was successfully retargeted to detect a different resistance gene simply by swapping the probe, demonstrating broad adaptability. The platform showed strong discrimination between target and non-target sequences and reproducible performance across independently fabricated sensors, supporting its potential for scalable manufacturing of decentralized diagnostics.
items.citation: Wang KY, Schulze H, Lozano Sanchez P et al.. Graphene foam biosensors for molecular diagnostics of antimicrobial resistance and emerging pathogen threats.. Biosensors & bioelectronics. 2026

items.title: Diagnostics and novel laboratory approaches to combat <i>Neisseria gonorrhoeae</i> antimicrobial resistance.
items.summary: The paper reviews how diagnostic tools and laboratory methods can help address the growing problem of antimicrobial resistance in *Neisseria gonorrhoeae*, the bacterium responsible for gonorrhea. The authors examine recent advances in detecting resistance, improving surveillance, and guiding clinical management of the infection. Their central argument is that the rapid global spread of multidrug-resistant strains, including those resistant to the last-line antibiotic ceftriaxone, poses a serious threat to current control strategies. They contend that rapid diagnostics and novel laboratory approaches can enable resistance-guided therapy, reduce transmission, and help preserve both existing and newly approved antimicrobials. The review concludes that combining culture-based surveillance with molecular assays targeting genetic determinants of resistance offers the most comprehensive strategy for monitoring and responding to the evolving resistance landscape.
items.citation: Soge OO, Fifer H, Alexander S et al.. Diagnostics and novel laboratory approaches to combat <i>Neisseria gonorrhoeae</i> antimicrobial resistance.. Expert review of molecular diagnostics. 2026

items.title: Executive Summary: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
items.summary: The Surviving Sepsis Campaign developed comprehensive updated international guidelines for the identification and management of sepsis and septic shock in adult patients, covering evidence published through mid-2025. Using GRADE methodology, an international multidisciplinary panel evaluated and voted on a total of one hundred twenty-nine statements spanning screening, infection management, hemodynamic support, respiratory care, adjunctive therapies, and long-term outcomes. Notable new recommendations include the use of prehospital sepsis screening tools and prehospital antibiotic administration for patients with septic shock and prolonged transport times, selective digestive decontamination in low-resistance settings, and active fluid removal after the acute resuscitation phase. Key changes from the prior guidelines include a shift away from routine empiric antifungal therapy, support for either invasive or noninvasive blood pressure monitoring in septic shock, and a recommendation favoring crystalloids alone over supplemental albumin for fluid resuscitation, while several other recommendations were upgraded or downgraded in strength based on updated evidence synthesis.
items.citation: Hallie C. Prescott, Massimo Antonelli, Waleed Alhazzani et al.. Executive Summary: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026. Critical Care Medicine. 2026

items.title: Silver Nanoparticles in Antibacterial Research: Mechanisms, Applications, and Emerging Perspectives
items.summary: Silver nanoparticles have attracted considerable attention as potential alternatives to conventional antibiotics, particularly given the rising challenge of drug-resistant pathogens. This review examines the physicochemical properties of silver nanoparticles that underpin their antibacterial activity, including how particle shape, size, and surface characteristics influence their effectiveness and specificity. The antibacterial mechanisms discussed include silver ion release causing membrane disruption, generation of reactive oxygen species, inhibition of protein synthesis, and interference with DNA replication. The paper surveys applications across agriculture, biomedical, and environmental fields, while also addressing the toxicity concerns that arise from inappropriate dosing or suboptimal formulation. The authors conclude by outlining future perspectives for safer and more effective deployment of silver nanoparticles as antibacterial agents.
items.citation: Hasan Karataş, Furkan Eker, Emir Akdaşçi et al.. Silver Nanoparticles in Antibacterial Research: Mechanisms, Applications, and Emerging Perspectives. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Inflammatory bowel diseases: pathological mechanisms and therapeutic perspectives
items.summary: This review paper examines the pathological mechanisms underlying inflammatory bowel disease, a heterogeneous group of chronic relapsing intestinal conditions encompassing Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, whose global incidence is rising alongside industrialization. The authors synthesize current understanding of IBD pathogenesis, focusing on intestinal mucosal immune dysregulation, barrier damage, gut microbiota dysbiosis, reactive oxygen species homeostasis imbalance, and the interplay between genetic and environmental factors. The review also addresses the substantial clinical challenge that a significant proportion of patients fail to respond adequately to existing biologics and small-molecule therapies, or develop side effects with prolonged use. Emerging therapeutic strategies discussed include reactive oxygen species modulators, novel immune-targeted approaches, intestinal barrier repair agents, microbiota-directed interventions, multi-omics-based precision medicine, and artificial intelligence-assisted therapy. The authors argue that these approaches collectively represent a pathway toward personalized precision medicine that could bridge the gap between basic research and clinical practice in IBD management.
items.citation: Xiaona Yang, Hong Guo, Min Zou. Inflammatory bowel diseases: pathological mechanisms and therapeutic perspectives. Molecular Biomedicine. 2026

items.title: Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation
items.summary: This review examines the role of neutrophils in chronic inflammation, challenging the traditional view that these innate immune cells function solely in acute, short-term defense responses. The authors synthesize current knowledge on how neutrophils, through mechanisms including neutrophil extracellular trap formation, reactive oxygen species production, degranulation, and phagocytosis, can adopt a hyperinflammatory phenotype that drives sustained tissue damage rather than resolving infection. The review also details how neutrophils actively crosstalk with other immune and non-immune cell types—including endothelial cells, monocytes, platelets, and lymphocytes—to orchestrate broader inflammatory and tissue remodeling processes. The second portion of the review applies this framework to specific immune-driven diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis, atherosclerosis, inflammatory bowel disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and fibrotic disorders. The authors conclude that deeper understanding of neutrophil behavior in chronic inflammatory contexts may reveal novel therapeutic targets for these conditions.
items.citation: Kaat Torfs, Gaël Vermeersch, Mieke Gouwy et al.. Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation. Cellular and Molecular Immunology. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and how its dysregulation contributes to a broad range of diseases. The authors synthesize the historical development of lipid metabolism research while focusing on how immune cells, including macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils, depend on lipid metabolic processes to drive both innate and adaptive immune responses. The review explores lipid functions, transport, and signaling pathways, with particular attention to how lipid metabolites regulate immune cell proliferation, differentiation, and activation across genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational levels. The authors further discuss how aberrant lipid metabolism underlies conditions such as autoimmune diseases, cancer, neurodegeneration, cardiovascular disease, and metabolic disorders. Finally, the review outlines current and emerging therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, including lipid-derived biomarkers and novel treatment approaches informed by ongoing clinical trials.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the relationship between the microbiota and cancer, covering how microbial communities influence carcinogenesis, tumor progression, and patient prognosis. The authors trace the historical development of the field from early observations of microbes in tumors to discoveries linking specific microorganisms to cancer initiation, and they analyze the molecular mechanisms through which the microbiota modulates oncogenic pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual nature of the microbiota as both a promoter and inhibitor of tumor growth, and explores how microbial communities mediate responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Therapeutic strategies targeting the microbiota—including probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—are surveyed alongside approved drugs and ongoing clinical trials. The authors conclude by emphasizing the need for personalized, microbiota-targeted approaches to advance oncological research and clinical applications.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Program-as-Weights: A Programming Paradigm for Fuzzy Functions
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge that many practical programming tasks—such as filtering log lines, fixing malformed data, or ranking search results—are difficult to implement with explicit rules and are increasingly delegated to large language model APIs, which sacrifice reproducibility, locality, and cost efficiency. The authors propose a paradigm called fuzzy-function programming, in which a natural-language specification is compiled into a compact neural artifact that runs locally. Their instantiation, Program-as-Weights, uses a compiler model trained on a large purpose-built dataset to generate parameter-efficient adapters for a frozen, lightweight interpreter model. The resulting system, using a small interpreter, achieves performance comparable to direct prompting of a much larger model while requiring far less inference memory and running efficiently on consumer hardware. This approach reframes large foundation models as one-time tool builders rather than per-query solvers, producing reusable artifacts that make subsequent function calls cheap and offline.
items.citation: Wentao Zhang, Liliana Hotsko, Woojeong Kim et al.. Program-as-Weights: A Programming Paradigm for Fuzzy Functions. arXiv. 2026

items.title: The Debris Disk Host $β$ Piscis Austrinus is a Rapidly Rotating Star Seen Nearly Pole-On
items.summary: This paper investigated the stellar properties and orientation of β Piscis Austrinus, a debris disk host star whose unusual spectral line shapes had long suggested it might be a rapidly rotating star viewed nearly pole-on. Using high-dispersion spectra modeled with a spectral synthesis code, along with iron line ratio constraints on surface temperature, the authors determined that the star is indeed oriented at a very low inclination and rotates at a rate close to its critical velocity, causing substantial gravity darkening that makes the polar regions significantly hotter than the equatorial regions. This rapid rotation also causes the star's apparent luminosity to be nearly half again larger than its true luminosity, illustrating how pole-on viewing geometry can significantly bias stellar characterization. The technique was validated by successfully recovering the known nearly pole-on orientation of Vega from spectroscopy alone, and the derived age of β PsA is consistent with that inferred independently from its companion star using lithium depletion models. The study demonstrates that spectroscopy alone can identify and characterize rapidly rotating stars seen nearly pole-on, without requiring interferometric observations.
items.citation: Colin Kane, Russel White, Jeremy Jones et al.. The Debris Disk Host $β$ Piscis Austrinus is a Rapidly Rotating Star Seen Nearly Pole-On. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Topological Control of Quantum Chaos Diagnostics: OTOCs, Spectral Statistics, and Information Scrambling in Ising Model
items.summary: This paper investigates how the underlying network topology of spin interactions governs the transition from integrable to chaotic quantum dynamics in Ising spin systems. The authors formulate the problem graph-theoretically, representing spins as vertices and interactions through adjacency matrices, and compare three network architectures — path, Erdős–Rényi, and Watts–Strogatz topologies — to isolate the role of long-range couplings and degree heterogeneity. Chaos and information scrambling are diagnosed through a suite of complementary tools including out-of-time-order correlators, Krylov complexity, bipartite and tripartite mutual information, level spacing statistics, and the spectral form factor. The study finds that heterogeneous, long-range network connectivity dramatically accelerates quantum information propagation, drives tripartite information to large negative values indicative of deep scrambling, and shifts level statistics from Poissonian to Wigner–Dyson distributions, with the Thouless time serving as a unifying indicator that correlates with the onset of scrambling. The work thereby establishes a unified framework connecting network topology to spectral, operator-growth, and information-theoretic signatures of quantum chaos and thermalization in many-body systems.
items.citation: Reza Pirmoradian, Soheir Rouhani, M. Reza Tanhayi. Topological Control of Quantum Chaos Diagnostics: OTOCs, Spectral Statistics, and Information Scrambling in Ising Model. arXiv. 2026

items.title: EvoPolicyGym: Evaluating Autonomous Policy Evolution in Interactive Environments
items.summary: This paper introduces a framework for evaluating how well autonomous agents can iteratively improve executable policies through feedback in interactive environments. The authors define a controlled setting called Autonomous Policy Evolution, in which an agent repeatedly edits a policy system within a fixed interaction budget, and instantiate it as a benchmark called EvoPolicyGym built from compact reinforcement learning environments. Unlike prior evaluations that reduce agent performance to a single final score or conflate it with open-ended software engineering, this benchmark captures trajectory-level diagnostics revealing how agents allocate their budget and convert feedback into parametric improvements. Testing multiple models on the benchmark, one language model achieved the strongest aggregate performance across all evaluated environments. The findings suggest that effective autonomous policy evolution depends not only on winning individual tasks but on discovering task-appropriate mechanisms and refining policies under bounded feedback conditions.
items.citation: Zhilin Wang, Han Song, Runzhe Zhan et al.. EvoPolicyGym: Evaluating Autonomous Policy Evolution in Interactive Environments. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Native-Opacity Sensitivity of a Fixed Delta Cephei MESA-RSP Pulsation Model
items.summary: This paper investigated how the choice of radiative opacity tables affects the modeled pulsation behavior of a classical Cepheid variable star, using a fixed stellar model of delta Cephei as a controlled test case. The researchers held all stellar and pulsation parameters constant while systematically varying only the high-temperature opacity source among three native configurations available within the MESA Radial Stellar Pulsation module, allowing the effect of the opacity table alone to be isolated. They found that the choice of opacity measurably influences key pulsation diagnostics including the period, amplitude growth rates, and fractional kinetic energy growth per cycle, with one configuration providing the closest match to the observed period of delta Cephei and another yielding substantially larger amplitude-growth diagnostics. The different opacity tables also produced systematically different period values, with one configuration giving a notably longer period than the others. Despite these differences, none of the tested opacity configurations on their own resolved the known discrepancy between modeled and observed pulsation amplitudes.
items.citation: Zuhoor Elahi, Christopher Sirola, Wafa Gull. Native-Opacity Sensitivity of a Fixed Delta Cephei MESA-RSP Pulsation Model. arXiv. 2026

items.title: MARVEL: Margin-Aware Robust von Mises-Fischer Expert Learning for Long-Tailed Out-of-Distribution Detection
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of out-of-distribution detection in automated clinical diagnostic systems, where deep learning models often assign high confidence to unfamiliar inputs they should reject.
