The authors survey emerging methods that combine cell-free DNA signals with circulating tumor cells and extracellular vesicles, highlighting sequencing and error-suppression strategies alongside fragmentomic and methylation features that retain tissue and chromatin context at low tumor fractions. They assess growing evidence supporting multi-cancer early detection, tissue-of-origin inference, and longitudinal surveillance, particularly when liquid biopsy signals are combined with radiomics and deep learning to improve risk stratification and positive predictive value while reducing unnecessary procedures. Based on this synthesis, the authors propose a pathway-aware clinical workflow beginning with blood-based risk scoring, followed by organ-directed imaging and targeted secondary testing, and they advocate for standardized reporting that includes calibration, decision-curve analysis, and external validation metrics to ensure generalizability. The review concludes that liquid biopsy is transitioning from exploratory signal discovery toward deployable clinical decision systems, contingent on standardized workflows, robust error suppression, and prospective real-world evaluation.
items.citation: Hanyu Zhu, Zhenyu Li, Kunxin Xie et al.. Liquid Biopsy in Early Screening of Cancers: Emerging Technologies and New Prospects. Biomedicines. 2026

items.title: From SRA to Self-Flow: Data Augmentation or Self-Supervision?
items.summary: This paper investigates the mechanism underlying performance improvements in self-alignment methods for diffusion transformer training, specifically examining why Self-Flow outperforms SRA. The authors questioned Self-Flow's own explanation — that gains arise from interactions between tokens at different noise levels, where cleaner tokens assist in denoising noisier ones — and instead hypothesized that the benefit stems from data augmentation along the noise dimension. To test this, they introduced a technique called Attention Separation, which maintains the same dual-timestep input structure as Self-Flow but blocks cross-attention between tokens at different noise levels, thereby isolating the contribution of token interaction from that of augmentation. They found that removing token interaction did not harm performance and could even improve it, indicating that data augmentation rather than cross-noise-level token interaction is the primary driver of Self-Flow's gains. Building on this insight, they combined self-representation alignment with dual-timestep and attention-separation augmentation, demonstrating the effectiveness of this unified approach on a standard image generation benchmark.
items.citation: Dengyang Jiang, Mengmeng Wang, Harry Yang et al.. From SRA to Self-Flow: Data Augmentation or Self-Supervision?. arXiv. 2026

items.title: VT-WAM: Visual-Tactile World Action Model for Contact-Rich Manipulation
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of contact-rich robotic manipulation, where critical tactile signals such as deformation, pressure, slip, and friction are temporally sparse and not captured by visual observations alone. The authors introduce VT-WAM, a Visual-Tactile World Action Model that simultaneously learns to predict future visual states, tactile deformation dynamics, and actions within a unified flow matching framework. The model incorporates two key components: an Asymmetric Mixture-of-Transformers attention mechanism that connects a static visual anchor with evolving tactile dynamics, and a contact-gated attention guidance module that directs action prediction to rely on tactile evidence specifically during contact phases. Evaluated across six real-world contact-rich manipulation tasks, VT-WAM substantially outperforms existing visual-tactile policy baselines in average success rate. Ablation studies confirm that explicitly modeling tactile deformation dynamics and enforcing contact-phase tactile attention are both essential contributors to the system's performance.
items.citation: Shuai Tian, Yupeng Zheng, Yuhang Zheng et al.. VT-WAM: Visual-Tactile World Action Model for Contact-Rich Manipulation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of building traffic simulation agents that both realistically imitate recorded driving behavior and can be deliberately steered along meaningful behavioral dimensions, such as speed, acceleration, and safety. The authors developed Controllable Neural Variational Agents, a framework that infers per-agent behavior latents from discounted returns using a variational approach, and combines this with a rectified-flow trajectory generator trained with classifier-free guidance. A key methodological contribution is the introduction of soft eligibility gates, which replace hard binary reward thresholds with smooth exponential decay to preserve gradient signals for agents near reward boundaries. Evaluated on a large open motion dataset, the framework achieves competitive realism while offering per-channel controllability absent from higher-ranked pure imitation models, with speed and acceleration steering producing well-behaved monotone responses and safety controllability substantially improved by the soft eligibility mechanism. The work also highlights that steering evaluations must be interpreted alongside physical plausibility checks to avoid reward-hacking artifacts.
items.citation: Juanwu Lu, Junyu Zhu, Ziran Wang. Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Interpretation-Oriented Cloud Removal via Observation-Anchored Residual Flow with Geo-Contextual Alignment
items.summary: This paper addresses the problem of cloud removal in optical remote sensing imagery, specifically targeting the gap between visual quality and semantic usefulness for downstream analytical tasks. The authors propose a framework called Geo-Anchored Cloud Removal, which reformulates cloud removal as a residual inversion process anchored to the cloudy observation rather than pure noise, enabling more stable and faithful reconstruction. A complementary module leverages a Vision Foundation Model to align reconstructions with a semantic manifold, preserving the spatial and semantic structures needed for tasks like segmentation and change detection. Experiments across multiple cloud removal datasets and a broad range of downstream tasks show that the proposed approach achieves better reconstruction quality while also improving performance on subsequent interpretation tasks compared to existing methods.
items.citation: Ziyao Wang, Maonan Wang, Yucheng He et al.. Interpretation-Oriented Cloud Removal via Observation-Anchored Residual Flow with Geo-Contextual Alignment. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Topological Control of Quantum Chaos Diagnostics: OTOCs, Spectral Statistics, and Information Scrambling in Ising Model
items.summary: This paper investigates how the topology of spin interaction networks governs the transition from integrable to chaotic quantum dynamics in Ising models. The authors reformulate the problem using graph theory, representing spins as vertices and interactions through adjacency matrices drawn from three distinct network topologies — path graphs, Erdős–Rényi random graphs, and Watts–Strogatz small-world graphs — and tune non-local coupling strengths and field heterogeneity to drive integrability breaking. To characterize the resulting quantum chaos, they employ a suite of complementary diagnostics including out-of-time-order correlators, Krylov complexity, bipartite and tripartite mutual information, level spacing statistics, and the spectral form factor. They find that long-range couplings and heterogeneous degree distributions dramatically accelerate information scrambling, as evidenced by large negative tripartite information, exponential OTOC growth with topology-dependent Lyapunov exponents, rapid Krylov complexity growth, and a crossover from Poissonian to Wigner–Dyson level statistics. A key finding is that a reduced Thouless time strongly correlates with faster informational and operator scrambling, establishing a unified framework connecting network topology to thermalization and non-equilibrium dynamics in quantum many-body systems.
items.citation: Reza Pirmoradian, Soheir Rouhani, M. Reza Tanhayi. Topological Control of Quantum Chaos Diagnostics: OTOCs, Spectral Statistics, and Information Scrambling in Ising Model. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Nonholonomic Source Seeking by Torque Tuning: Local and Semi-Global Feedbacks
items.summary: This paper investigates the problem of steering a torque-controlled nonholonomic vehicle toward an unknown source using only a laterally displaced scalar sensor, without access to position, heading, gradient, or source-location information. The vehicle maintains constant forward speed while yaw motion is governed by a torque input with unknown inertia and damping parameters. Two torque control laws are proposed, each combining a fast oscillatory component that produces averaged steering via symmetric-product approximation with a slowly tuned bias component that selects the desired orbit around the source. The first design relies solely on the scalar sensor measurement and employs a Lie-bracket extremum-seeking update, achieving local practical stability, while the second incorporates forward-speed and yaw-rate measurements to tune the bias through a yaw-rate tracking error, yielding a globally asymptotically stable averaged system and semi-global practical stability of the full system. Simulations are provided to demonstrate the effectiveness of both designs.
items.citation: Bo Wang. Nonholonomic Source Seeking by Torque Tuning: Local and Semi-Global Feedbacks. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent innovations in microfluidic chip technology and their applications in laboratory medicine, with particular attention to advances in chip design, fabrication, and biosensor integration. The paper surveys developments in manufacturing approaches such as three-dimensional printing and flexible modular substrates, which have improved device performance, sensitivity, and clinical utility while enabling miniaturized processing of complex assays from minimal sample volumes. Advanced biosensor integration now supports real-time detection of circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes, extending the utility of these platforms to cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, point-of-care testing, and personalized medicine. A central theme is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has produced smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support. The review also identifies ongoing challenges in manufacturing consistency, clinical validation, and system integration, and outlines future directions for translating these technologies into routine clinical and public health practice.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Feasibility of integrating a point of care triage test into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique: a qualitative evaluation
items.summary: This study explored whether a point-of-care triage test measuring interferon-gamma-induced protein 10, a chemokine correlated with HIV viral load, could feasibly be integrated into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique. Using a qualitative approach nested within a clinical evaluation at two primary healthcare facilities, the researchers conducted multiple rounds of interviews with people living with HIV on treatment, healthcare providers, and programme stakeholders to capture perceptions of the test and its implementation considerations. Participants widely valued viral load monitoring but identified significant barriers to current practice, including laboratory delays, access difficulties, and health system constraints. The point-of-care triage test was broadly acceptable across all groups, with same-day results perceived to reduce patient anxiety, support treatment adherence, and enable timely clinical decisions, while providers highlighted its potential to prioritise those needing confirmatory viral load testing. Successful implementation was seen as contingent on adequate staff training, workflow integration, quality assurance, and clear patient communication to address concerns about test accuracy and the continued necessity of confirmatory testing.
items.citation: Myburgh H, Saura-Lázaro A, van den Bogaart E et al.. Feasibility of integrating a point of care triage test into routine antiretroviral therapy monitoring in Mozambique: a qualitative evaluation. 2026

items.title: Implementation bottlenecks of near point of care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study.
items.summary: This study investigated the bottlenecks encountered when implementing near point-of-care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people living with HIV in Tanzania, as part of a larger multi-country cluster-randomised trial. Researchers conducted clinic observations and in-depth interviews with healthcare workers at multiple time points across intervention sites, using a combined deductive and inductive thematic framework analysis. The findings showed that near point-of-care testing improved result turnaround times and enabled same-day counselling, which motivated both healthcare workers and patients, while compatibility with existing workflows, teamwork, and management support acted as key facilitators. However, significant barriers included prolonged patient waiting times, shortages of physical infrastructure such as dedicated rooms and reliable electricity, staff shortages, and delays caused by transporting samples to nearby laboratories. The authors conclude that realising the full benefits of near point-of-care viral load monitoring requires targeted investment in staff training, infrastructure, sample handling processes, and alignment with clinical guidelines.
items.citation: Msoka P, Mtenga A, Maro R et al.. Implementation bottlenecks of near point of care HIV viral load monitoring for children and young people in Tanzania: A Qualitative Study.. PloS one. 2026

items.title: Baseline Data on Viral Suppression, Treatment Adherence and Mental Health in Peripartum Girls and Young Women With HIV in Zimbabwe.
items.summary: This study examined viral suppression, treatment adherence, drug exposure, and mental health among peripartum adolescent girls and young women living with HIV in Zimbabwe, a population known to have poorer viral control than older women. Researchers enrolled participants aged fifteen to twenty-four years who were peripartum and on tenofovir-lamivudine-dolutegravir regimens, collecting point-of-care viral load measurements, urine tenofovir assays, and validated mental health assessments at baseline. The study found high rates of viral suppression at enrollment, with detectable urine tenofovir and higher self-reported adherence being the strongest predictors of suppression, while food and financial security and marital status were also significantly associated. Mental health outcomes were largely positive, with most participants reporting minimal depression and anxiety, though moderate HIV-related stigma was prevalent in over half the cohort. These baseline findings suggest that dolutegravir-based regimens are achieving strong viral control in this group, and that adherence support alongside attention to social determinants of health may be key to sustaining these outcomes.
items.venue: Journal of the International AIDS Society
items.citation: Chawana-Mutingwende TD, Ngara B, Mudadi LS et al.. Baseline Data on Viral Suppression, Treatment Adherence and Mental Health in Peripartum Girls and Young Women With HIV in Zimbabwe.. Journal of the International AIDS Society. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and how its dysregulation contributes to a broad range of diseases. The authors synthesize the research history of lipid metabolism and focus particularly on how immune cells, including macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils, depend on lipid metabolic processes for proliferation, differentiation, and activation across both innate and adaptive immunity. The review discusses lipid functions, transport, and signaling pathways, and explores multilevel regulatory mechanisms spanning genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational levels. The authors further address how lipid metabolic dysregulation underpins conditions such as autoimmune diseases, cancer, neurodegeneration, cardiovascular disease, aging, and metabolic disorders. Finally, the review surveys therapeutic strategies targeting lipid metabolism, ongoing clinical trials, and emerging opportunities involving lipid-derived biomarkers and novel treatment approaches.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Sepsis
items.citation: Mervyn Singer, Derek C Angus, Djillali Annane et al.. Sepsis. The Lancet. 2026

items.title: Promoting Human Milk and Breastfeeding for the Very Low Birth Weight Infant: Clinical Report
items.summary: This clinical report examines best practices for promoting human milk and breastfeeding in very low birth weight infants cared for in the neonatal intensive care unit. The report synthesizes evidence on the nutritional and health benefits of mother's own milk, the role of pasteurized donor human milk as an alternative when mother's own milk is unavailable or insufficient, and the circumstances under which preterm infant formula is appropriate. The authors recommend intensive, individualized lactation support throughout hospitalization, including early and frequent milk expression, skin-to-skin care, access to effective breast pumps, and multidisciplinary adoption of breastfeeding practices. The report also emphasizes that addressing social inequities requires equitable access to donor milk, peer support, interpreter services, and systematic identification of families' unmet basic needs.
items.citation: Margaret G. Parker, Lisa M. Stellwagen, Emily R. Miller et al.. Promoting Human Milk and Breastfeeding for the Very Low Birth Weight Infant: Clinical Report. PEDIATRICS. 2026

items.title: CD4+ T cells mediate CAR-T cell-associated immune-related adverse events after BCMA CAR-T cell therapy
items.citation: Matthew Ho, Luca Paruzzo, Julia Han Noll et al.. CD4+ T cells mediate CAR-T cell-associated immune-related adverse events after BCMA CAR-T cell therapy. Nature Medicine. 2026

items.title: Exosomal bioactive molecules: molecular regulation, functional heterogeneity, and translational implications in disease diagnostics and therapeutics
items.summary: This review examines exosomes as nanoscale extracellular vesicles that mediate intercellular communication through the transfer of bioactive molecular cargo, including proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids. The authors synthesize current understanding of the molecular machinery governing exosome biogenesis and selective cargo loading, with particular attention to the roles of ESCRT complexes, lipid domains, RNA-binding proteins, and post-translational modifications in generating functional heterogeneity. The review surveys cutting-edge omics platforms and machine learning-based tools used to characterize exosomal content, and evaluates the diagnostic utility of exosomes as liquid biopsy biomarkers across cancer, metabolic, and neurodegenerative diseases. Therapeutic applications of both native and engineered exosomes are explored, including targeted drug delivery, wound healing, and neuroregeneration, alongside an overview of ongoing clinical trials. The authors identify persistent translational barriers such as isolation protocol variability, cargo standardization, and regulatory constraints, while proposing that emerging approaches including synthetic exosome mimetics and artificial intelligence-driven diagnostics will advance exosome-based precision medicine.
items.citation: Victor U. Chigozie. Exosomal bioactive molecules: molecular regulation, functional heterogeneity, and translational implications in disease diagnostics and therapeutics. Clinical and Experimental Medicine. 2026

items.title: Q-GAIN: A Python Package for Machine Learning and Physically Informed Analysis Applications
items.summary: This paper introduces Q-GAIN, a Python package designed to streamline the application of machine learning and physics-informed analysis methods to cold-atom experiments, particularly imaging of Bose-Einstein condensates. The package implements a modular workflow encompassing data loading, preprocessing, machine learning-based feature identification, and conventional physics analysis. The authors demonstrate the framework's versatility through three distinct tasks: classifying handwritten digits as a baseline demonstration, re-implementing a previously developed soliton detection tool within the new framework, and developing an object-detection system for identifying quantized vortices in images of ring-shaped condensates. The package supports classification, object detection, and physics-informed metrics out of the box, aiming to lower the barrier for deploying machine learning tools in experimental cold-atom research.
items.citation: M. Doris, S. Guo, S. M. Koh et al.. Q-GAIN: A Python Package for Machine Learning and Physically Informed Analysis Applications. arXiv. 2026

items.title: WattGPU: Predicting Inference Power and Latency on Unseen GPUs and LLMs
items.summary: WattGPU addresses the challenge of predicting GPU power consumption and inference latency for large language model workloads without requiring exhaustive hardware profiling. The system introduces two predictive models that rely exclusively on publicly available LLM metadata and GPU specifications, enabling generalization to hardware and models not seen during training. The models were evaluated using leave-one-GPU-out and leave-one-LLM-out cross-validation across a dataset spanning dozens of open-source LLMs and multiple server-grade NVIDIA GPUs under both offline and server deployment scenarios. The power draw model and latency model both achieved low median absolute percentage errors on unseen configurations, with strong GPU ranking correlations that are useful for deployment optimization decisions. Compared to physically grounded baselines such as load-scaled thermal design power and roofline models, WattGPU substantially reduced prediction error on unseen LLM–GPU combinations, demonstrating that metadata-driven prediction is a viable alternative to hardware profiling for energy-aware deployment planning.
items.citation: Mauricio Fadel Argerich, Jonathan Fürst, Marta Patiño-Martínez. WattGPU: Predicting Inference Power and Latency on Unseen GPUs and LLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Developers' Experience with Generative AI Beyond Productivity Assessment -- Insights from an Empirical Mixed-Methods Field Study
items.summary: This paper investigated how professional developers experience and interact with generative AI coding assistants in their natural work environments, going beyond typical productivity metrics to examine well-being, cognitive load, and interaction preferences. The researchers conducted an empirical mixed-methods field study that combined controlled data collection sessions with natural work periods to capture authentic usage patterns. Developers reported general satisfaction with generative AI, particularly for repetitive and structured tasks, and perceived gains in efficiency and productivity. A key finding was that different interaction modes — in-code suggestions versus chat-based prompting — each independently improved task efficiency and reduced perceived workload, but combining both modes within a single task undermined these benefits, leading the authors to propose a rule-of-thumb for choosing interaction types based on task characteristics and complexity. The study also found that during development-heavy tasks, AI interaction itself introduced perceived cognitive load, that perceived productivity depended heavily on output quality, and that participation in the study enhanced developers' awareness and intentional use of these tools.
items.citation: Charlotte Brandebusemeyer, Kerim Zunic, Thomas Zimmermann et al.. Developers' Experience with Generative AI Beyond Productivity Assessment -- Insights from an Empirical Mixed-Methods Field Study. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Actuator Reality Shaping for Zero-Shot Sim-to-Real Robot Learning
items.summary: This paper addresses the sim-to-real transfer problem in robot learning, which arises because physical actuators behave in complex, nonlinear ways that differ from the idealized dynamics assumed during simulation-based policy training. Rather than improving simulator accuracy through system identification or domain randomization, the authors propose a fundamentally different approach called actuator reality shaping, which instead modifies the physical actuators' closed-loop behavior to match the simple second-order dynamics used in simulation. This is achieved by equipping each joint with a two-degree-of-freedom feedforward-feedback controller that separates reference tracking from disturbance rejection, creating a standardized actuator interface that policies can rely on without adaptation. The method was validated across multiple robotic platforms, including a single-joint servo, a multi-degree-of-freedom robotic arm, a wheeled-legged robot, and a humanoid walker, consistently reducing sim-to-real tracking error and improving zero-shot task performance compared to standard control baselines and sim-to-real alternatives. The results suggest that shaping actuator behavior to conform to simulation assumptions can serve as a broadly reusable, hardware-agnostic interface for deploying learned policies without task-level fine-tuning.
items.citation: Satoshi Yamamori, Koji Ishihara, Kentaro Minamikawa et al.. Actuator Reality Shaping for Zero-Shot Sim-to-Real Robot Learning. arXiv. 2026

items.title: A Time-to-Boundary Margin for Transient Stability: Unifying Critical Clearing Time and Operating-Point Drift
items.summary: This paper develops a transient-stability margin analogous to the well-established loading margin used in voltage stability analysis, reframing the problem as a time-based rather than distance-based measure. The proposed margin is defined as the first-passage time of the joint state-parameter motion to the synchronism boundary, unifying two phenomena typically treated separately: the critical clearing time under fixed operating conditions and the slow drift of the operating point toward a static loadability limit. The authors prove that this margin equals the critical clearing time exactly in the one-machine-infinite-bus reduction and verify this result against a published benchmark with negligible deviation. On a standard multimachine test system, a single-machine-equivalent reduction reproduces the critical clearing time within a tight conservative bound, and a critical slowing-down signature is shown to indicate proximity to the stability boundary. For the general multimachine case, the paper identifies the controlling unstable equilibrium as the principal obstacle to obtaining a fully certified margin, while characterizing the transfer-conductance contribution as tractably boundable.
items.citation: Marián Mešter. A Time-to-Boundary Margin for Transient Stability: Unifying Critical Clearing Time and Operating-Point Drift. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Towards Load-Aware Prefill Deflection for Disaggregated LLM Serving
items.summary: This paper addresses a key inefficiency in disaggregated large language model serving, where prefill and decode phases run on separate GPU pools to avoid interference but can create load imbalances under bursty workloads. The authors found that in this setting, queuing delays and inter-node key-value cache transfers dominate tail latency for time-to-first-token, with actual prefill execution accounting for only a small fraction of the bottleneck. To address this, they developed a proactive scheduling approach that redirects prefill work to underutilized decode nodes, executing it in interleaved chunks alongside ongoing decode batches while respecting per-token latency constraints. By running the prefill phase directly on the decode node, the approach eliminates the costly inter-node key-value cache transfer entirely. Evaluated on production-style traces using a real model, the scheduler achieves substantial reductions in tail time-to-first-token and large improvements in service-level objective attainment compared to existing disaggregated schedulers, with routing decisions made at sub-millisecond cost.
items.citation: Shrikara Arun, Anjaly Parayil, Srikant Bharadwaj et al.. Towards Load-Aware Prefill Deflection for Disaggregated LLM Serving. arXiv. 2026

items.title: 'It's Primarily Around Their Viral Load': Public Health Decision-Making and HIV Risk Assemblages in Ontario, Canada.
items.summary: This study examined how public health practitioners in Ontario, Canada determine when the behaviors of people living with HIV constitute significant risk warranting intervention, including the potential use of legally enforceable orders. Using assemblage theory as a framework, the researchers conducted semi-structured interviews with public health personnel and analyzed the data through reflexive thematic analysis. The findings revealed that HIV risk assessments are shaped by an interplay of biomedical markers such as viral load, care continuity, co-infection status, compliance with directives, and evolving scientific discourses, rather than by individual behavior alone. The principle of Undetectable = Untransmittable had partially reshaped how risk was understood, functioning as a governance tool that could neutralize perceived risk, but its adoption was inconsistent across public health units, resulting in variable and sometimes coercive responses toward people living with HIV. The authors argue that conceptualizing HIV risk as relationally produced rather than individually determined challenges punitive approaches, and they call for equity-based public health practices and more consistent uptake of current evidence to avoid discriminatory outcomes.
items.citation: Daroya E, French M, Hastings C et al.. 'It's Primarily Around Their Viral Load': Public Health Decision-Making and HIV Risk Assemblages in Ontario, Canada.. Sociology of health & illness. 2026

items.title: Durable viral suppression in the dolutegravir era: a data-enabled, risk-stratified model for integrated HIV care in Uganda.
items.summary: This paper examined persistent gaps in HIV viral suppression and disease management in Uganda despite near-universal adoption of dolutegravir-based antiretroviral therapy, recognizing that viral non-suppression, advanced HIV disease, and co-occurring tuberculosis and non-communicable diseases remain significant challenges. The researchers analyzed a large routine-care cohort from a major Ugandan HIV support organization spanning a decade, and conducted a regional systematic review and meta-analysis to characterize the burden and distribution of viral non-suppression across East Africa. They found that viral non-suppression clustered in predictable social and clinical risk groups, with the regional estimate substantially higher than that observed in the cohort, indicating that regimen potency alone is insufficient to close remaining gaps. In response, the authors propose a pragmatic, data-enabled, risk-stratified care model that uses routine electronic health record signals to trigger timely viral load follow-up, deliver targeted adherence and socioeconomic support bundles to high-risk clients, and integrate tuberculosis and non-communicable disease management within HIV care platforms. The authors argue that this accountable, cascade-oriented approach, tracked through a focused operational dashboard, can translate antiretroviral scale-up into durable suppression and accelerate progress toward global HIV treatment targets.
items.citation: Namaganda MM, Nabende JN, Kateete DP et al.. Durable viral suppression in the dolutegravir era: a data-enabled, risk-stratified model for integrated HIV care in Uganda.. Frontiers in public health. 2026

items.title: Optimizing hepatitis B diagnosis for mothers in a low-resource setting: A field pilot of Xpert point-of-care viral load testing in Ugandan antenatal clinics.
items.summary: This study investigated whether point-of-care hepatitis B viral load testing using the Xpert HBV DNA assay could be feasibly implemented in antenatal care settings in Uganda to support timely antiviral prophylaxis for pregnant women. Conducted across ten high-volume hospitals in a two-phase design, the research first verified the diagnostic accuracy of the Xpert platform against a standard centralized reference method, then assessed its operational integration, usability, and cost within routine antenatal care. The Xpert assay showed full concordance with the reference platform, the vast majority of samples were processed and results returned to providers, and a substantial proportion of mothers received results on the same day of testing. The cost per test was comparable to centralized testing while offering meaningful advantages in turnaround time and reduced patient visits, though challenges such as supply stockouts and competing laboratory priorities were noted. The authors conclude that point-of-care hepatitis B viral load testing is an accurate, timely, and operationally feasible approach for improving prevention of mother-to-child transmission in resource-limited settings like Uganda.
items.citation: Nabitaka LK, Kasone V, Olal E et al.. Optimizing hepatitis B diagnosis for mothers in a low-resource setting: A field pilot of Xpert point-of-care viral load testing in Ugandan antenatal clinics.. PLOS global public health. 2026

items.title: Comparative evaluation of a multi-antigen malaria rapid diagnostic test against qPCR and microscopy in a co-endemic area of Southern Ethiopia.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a multi-antigen rapid diagnostic test for malaria in a region of southern Ethiopia where both *Plasmodium falciparum* and *P. vivax* co-circulate, and where limitations of conventional HRP2-based tests are well recognised. Febrile patients attending health facilities during peak transmission season were tested using the new Bioline Malaria multi-antigen RDT, which detects HRP2 and dual pLDH markers, alongside expert microscopy and quantitative PCR as reference standards. Malaria prevalence was highest when measured by qPCR, which also identified more mixed-species and low-density infections than either microscopy or the RDT. The multi-antigen RDT showed high specificity and strong agreement with microscopy, but its sensitivity declined notably when qPCR served as the reference, indicating that submicroscopic infections were frequently missed. The authors conclude that while the RDT performs well for routine clinical diagnosis, more sensitive tools are needed for surveillance and control in this endemic setting.
items.citation: Bogale A, Mitiku A, Tefera S et al.. Comparative evaluation of a multi-antigen malaria rapid diagnostic test against qPCR and microscopy in a co-endemic area of Southern Ethiopia.. Scientific reports. 2026

items.title: Characterization of arbovirus infections in patients within Haiti by screening discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes.
items.summary: This study investigated circulating arboviruses in Haiti during a period when conventional surveillance was disrupted by security concerns, using an unconventional sample source. Researchers extracted nucleic acids from discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes collected from febrile patients at healthcare centers across two Haitian departments over several years, then screened these materials using a series of real-time PCR assays targeting flaviviruses, alphaviruses, and Oropouche virus, followed by next-generation sequencing and phylogenetic analysis. Dengue virus serotype three was identified in a substantial number of samples, all collected in a single year, with genomic sequences showing close similarity to strains circulating across the Caribbean during the same period; no alphavirus or Oropouche virus infections were detected. The findings align with a broader dengue serotype three outbreak documented across the Americas during that time. The study demonstrates that discarded malaria rapid diagnostic tests can serve as a practical and valuable surveillance tool for arboviral pathogens in resource-limited or politically unstable settings where conventional public health infrastructure is compromised.
items.citation: Matulis GA, Smith HP, Hall G et al.. Characterization of arbovirus infections in patients within Haiti by screening discarded malaria rapid diagnostic test cassettes.. PLoS neglected tropical diseases. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the relationship between the microbiota and cancer, covering how microbial communities influence carcinogenesis, tumor progression, and patient prognosis. The authors trace the historical development of microbiota-cancer research and explore the molecular mechanisms by which microbiota modulate oncogenic pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review addresses the dual capacity of the microbiota to both promote and inhibit tumor growth, as well as its role in shaping responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Emerging therapeutic strategies targeting the microbiota—including probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—are discussed alongside approved drugs and ongoing clinical trials. The authors conclude by calling for personalized approaches to exploit the anticancer potential of the microbiota and propose a comprehensive framework for advancing microbiota-targeted oncology.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: ZigBee Based Low Latency IoT and AI Integrated Framework for Real Time Telehealth Monitoring
items.summary: This paper presents an integrated telehealth monitoring framework that combines IoT sensor networks with cloud-based artificial intelligence to enable continuous, real-time health surveillance for elderly and chronically ill patients. The system uses the ZigBee wireless protocol to transmit physiological data — including heart rate, body temperature, and oxygen levels — from wearable sensors to a centralized processing unit with minimal power consumption. Multiple machine learning algorithms, including random forest, support vector machine, and logistic regression, were applied to detect health anomalies, with the random forest model achieving particularly high accuracy and recall rates. Testing in a real-world care facility demonstrated low transmission latency and low power draw, suggesting suitability for long-term deployment. The authors claim their modular, scalable design addresses key limitations of existing telehealth systems, such as high delays and frequent false alarms, while reducing the monitoring burden on healthcare providers.
items.citation: B. V. Ravi Kumar, Mohammad Shahnawaz Shaikh. ZigBee Based Low Latency IoT and AI Integrated Framework for Real Time Telehealth Monitoring. Emerging Science Journal. 2026

items.title: Sepsis
items.citation: Mervyn Singer, Derek C Angus, Djillali Annane et al.. Sepsis. The Lancet. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review examines recent advances in microfluidic chip technology, commonly known as lab-on-a-chip systems, and their growing role in laboratory medicine and diagnostics. The authors conducted a comprehensive review of innovations in chip design and fabrication, including developments in three-dimensional printing, modular architectures, and flexible substrates, alongside progress in biosensor integration for detecting circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has enabled the creation of smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support. The review covers clinical applications spanning cancer diagnostics, infectious disease detection, point-of-care testing, personalized medicine, and emerging areas such as neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling. The authors also address persistent challenges in manufacturing, clinical validation, and system integration, and outline future directions for translating these technologies into routine clinical and public health practice.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Cancer workforce—a global crisis: a Lancet Oncology Commission
items.citation: Hedvig Hricak, Zachary J. Ward, Fabio Ynoe Moraes et al.. Cancer workforce—a global crisis: a Lancet Oncology Commission. The Lancet Oncology. 2026

items.title: Large language models in global health
items.summary: This paper examines the role of large language models in global health, with particular focus on their potential and limitations in low- and middle-income countries. The authors review current progress, case studies, and structural barriers, including inadequate digital infrastructure, scarcity of locally relevant data, underrepresentation of local languages, and limited in-country expertise and capacity-building resources. They find that mobile-based applications, hybrid artificial intelligence systems, and open-weight models show promise for improving access to care, diagnostics, and clinical decision-making in resource-constrained settings. However, the paper identifies significant risks such as model hallucinations, equity concerns, and environmental impacts, arguing that rigorous validation, localized fine-tuning, and robust global governance frameworks are essential. The authors conclude that with appropriate safeguards, strategic investment, and codevelopment partnerships, large language models could help bridge health system disparities and support underserved communities without perpetuating existing inequities.
items.citation: Jasmine Chiat Ling Ong, Yilin Ning, Rui Yang et al.. Large language models in global health. Nature Health. 2026

items.title: Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs
items.summary: The paper investigates methods for monitoring large language model outputs in real time to detect unsafe or incorrect generations during deployment. The authors propose a straightforward monitoring approach that converts a verifier signal from an external model into an alarm by applying a threshold, with the threshold calibrated using risk control principles. This simple design was evaluated on mathematical reasoning and adversarial red teaming datasets and compared against more sophisticated monitors based on sequential hypothesis testing. The findings indicate that this simple thresholding approach performs competitively with the more complex alternatives, suggesting that elaborate monitoring machinery may not always be necessary for effective online safety oversight.
items.citation: Mona Schirmer, Metod Jazbec, Alexander Timans et al.. Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation
items.summary: This paper investigates a limitation of on-policy self-distillation for training large language models to reason, specifically the problem of privileged information leakage, where a student model learns shortcuts dependent on answer information available to the teacher but absent at test time, leading to overfitting and poor generalization. To address this, the authors propose DemoPSD, a framework that selectively applies teacher guidance by steering the student toward a reverse-KL barycenter target—a weighted geometric combination of the teacher and student distributions—rather than fitting the full teacher distribution. The blending at each token position is adaptively controlled by measuring the discrepancy between the teacher and student distributions, allowing the model to preserve its own reasoning capacity while still benefiting from teacher supervision. The authors provide theoretical guarantees that DemoPSD attenuates privileged information leakage and preserves exploration capacity under dense token-level distillation. Experiments across multiple scientific domains demonstrate that DemoPSD outperforms existing self-distillation and reinforcement learning baselines, maintains higher training entropy, and generalizes more robustly to out-of-distribution benchmarks.
items.citation: Yunhe Li, Hao Shi, Wenhao Liu et al.. DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of building traffic simulation agents that both realistically imitate logged driving behavior and can be deliberately steered along interpretable behavioral dimensions such as speed, safety, and map compliance. The authors introduce Controllable Neural Variational Agents, a framework that infers per-agent latent behavior representations from discounted returns using a closed-form variational update, and uses these latents to condition a rectified-flow trajectory generator trained with classifier-free guidance via a mixed channel-mask curriculum. To address sparse reward signals, they develop soft eligibility gates that replace hard binary thresholds with smooth exponential decay, preserving gradient information for agents near reward boundaries. Evaluated on a large-scale open motion dataset, the approach achieves competitive realism while offering per-channel controllability that purely imitative higher-ranked models lack, with speed, acceleration, and safety controls producing well-behaved monotone responses. The work also highlights that controllability evaluations must be paired with physical-plausibility checks to avoid misinterpreting reward-hacking artifacts as genuine behavioral steering.
items.citation: Juanwu Lu, Junyu Zhu, Ziran Wang. Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: The Debris Disk Host $β$ Piscis Austrinus is a Rapidly Rotating Star Seen Nearly Pole-On
items.summary: The paper investigated β Piscis Austrinus, a debris disk host star whose unusual spectral line shapes had long suggested it might be a rapidly rotating star viewed nearly pole-on. Using high-dispersion spectroscopy and a dedicated spectral synthesis code, the authors modeled the star's photospheric properties, inclination, and rotation rate, with iron line ratios providing additional temperature constraints. The analysis confirmed an nearly pole-on orientation and highly rapid rotation approaching the critical rotation rate, resulting in substantial gravity darkening that makes the polar region significantly hotter than the equatorial region and causes the apparent luminosity to substantially exceed the true luminosity. The same methodology applied to Vega reproduced results consistent with interferometric measurements, validating the approach. Comparisons with stellar evolutionary models yielded a mass and age for β PsA that is consistent with the age estimated independently for its companion star, demonstrating that spectroscopy alone can characterize rapidly rotating pole-on stars.
items.citation: Colin Kane, Russel White, Jeremy Jones et al.. The Debris Disk Host $β$ Piscis Austrinus is a Rapidly Rotating Star Seen Nearly Pole-On. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Cryogenic RF characterization of the MAGO cavity for high-frequency gravitational-wave detection
items.summary: This paper investigated the use of superconducting radio-frequency cavities as resonant sensors for detecting gravitational waves in the kilohertz-to-megahertz frequency range. The researchers performed cryogenic radio-frequency characterization of a prototype superconducting niobium cavity with an unconventional geometry specifically designed to achieve narrow separation between electromagnetic modes. Testing was carried out at temperatures down to two kelvin, with mechanical tuning used to achieve a small mode splitting at cryogenic conditions. The measurements confirmed high electromagnetic quality factors and revealed phase transfer characteristics relevant to low-level RF control, but also uncovered mode coupling effects potentially caused by one-point multipacting, and mechanical quality factors significantly lower than theoretical predictions. The study demonstrates that established superconducting radio-frequency preparation and characterization techniques can be successfully applied to non-standard resonator geometries, providing key experimental data for the future development of gravitational-wave detectors based on this technology.
items.citation: Can Dokuyucu, Bianca Giaccone, Tom Krokotsch et al.. Cryogenic RF characterization of the MAGO cavity for high-frequency gravitational-wave detection. arXiv. 2026

items.title: TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of evaluating how well automated agents handle the co-evolution of software tests and code, where changes to source code should be accompanied by corresponding updates or additions to tests. The authors introduce TestEvo-Bench, a benchmark mined from real software repository commit histories that includes two distinct tracks: test generation, requiring agents to write new tests capturing changed behavior, and test update, requiring agents to adapt existing failing tests to modified code. Each task is paired with environment configuration to support execution-grounded evaluation metrics including pass rate, coverage, and mutation score, rather than relying on static metadata. A key design feature is that the benchmark is "live," with tasks timestamped and periodically refreshed via an automated pipeline, allowing evaluations to be restricted to tasks created after a model's training cutoff to mitigate data leakage. Experiments with several state-of-the-art agents combining strong harnesses and foundation models show respectable success rates overall, but performance degrades notably on the most recently added tasks and under constrained computational budgets, highlighting remaining challenges for test automation agents.
items.citation: Jiale Amber Wang, Kaiyuan Wang, Pengyu Nie. TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Emerging blood-based diagnostic strategies for tuberculosis.
items.summary: This review examines the landscape of blood-based diagnostic approaches for tuberculosis, a persistent global health burden caused by *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*. The authors survey current serological biomarkers being used or investigated for TB diagnosis, with particular attention to their value in detecting extrapulmonary TB and in patients who yield negative results from conventional sputum culture and molecular testing. The review also discusses how advanced diagnostic technologies can be integrated with these biomarkers to enhance performance. The authors conclude that serological diagnosis, driven by the continuous identification of novel biomarkers, offers a simple, rapid, and cost-effective complementary strategy with meaningful potential to improve TB diagnosis across varied clinical contexts.
items.citation: Yue C, Geng X, Sun X et al.. Emerging blood-based diagnostic strategies for tuberculosis.. Clinical chemistry and laboratory medicine. 2026

items.title: Diagnostic accuracy of GeneReady MTB assay on sputum for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis: a prospective multicenter study in China.
items.summary: This prospective multicenter study evaluated the diagnostic accuracy of a novel molecular assay, GeneReady MTB, for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis from sputum samples in Chinese hospitals. Participants with symptoms suggestive of active pulmonary tuberculosis underwent testing with GeneReady alongside smear microscopy, mycobacterial culture, and the established Xpert MTB/RIF assay, with microbiological culture serving as the reference standard. GeneReady achieved high sensitivity and specificity against the microbiological reference standard, with sensitivity being notably higher in smear-positive compared to smear-negative patients, reflecting the expected influence of bacterial load on molecular detection. The assay showed strong agreement with the Xpert MTB/RIF assay across both positive and negative results, demonstrating comparable diagnostic performance. The authors conclude that GeneReady's combination of rapid turnaround, lower cost, and ease of use positions it as a promising initial diagnostic tool for pulmonary tuberculosis, particularly in resource-limited settings where access to molecular testing is currently restricted.
items.citation: Shang Y, Tang Z, Deng Y et al.. Diagnostic accuracy of GeneReady MTB assay on sputum for detecting pulmonary tuberculosis: a prospective multicenter study in China.. Journal of clinical microbiology. 2026

items.title: Comparative Diagnostic Accuracy of GeneXpert MTB/RIF and TB-LAMP for Tuberculosis Detection Among Presumptive Tuberculosis Cases in Bayelsa State, Nigeria
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of two rapid nucleic acid amplification tests, GeneXpert MTB/RIF and TB-LAMP, for detecting tuberculosis in a high-burden Nigerian setting, using solid mycobacterial culture as the reference standard. A cross-sectional design was used, testing sputum samples from nearly four hundred presumptive tuberculosis cases across multiple health facilities in Bayelsa State simultaneously with both platforms and culture. TB-LAMP demonstrated higher sensitivity than GeneXpert but lower specificity, and its positivity rate significantly exceeded that of culture, partly attributable to false-positive results from non-tuberculous mycobacteria. GeneXpert showed strong concordance with culture and offered the added advantage of simultaneous rifampicin resistance detection, making it better suited as a confirmatory tool. The authors propose a sequential algorithm in which TB-LAMP serves as an initial low-cost screening test at peripheral facilities, followed by GeneXpert confirmation, to maximize case detection while managing resource constraints.
items.citation: UMUWE P, Wariso KT, ILOZUMBA J et al.. Comparative Diagnostic Accuracy of GeneXpert MTB/RIF and TB-LAMP for Tuberculosis Detection Among Presumptive Tuberculosis Cases in Bayelsa State, Nigeria. 2026

items.title: Field-ready portable rapid nucleic acid test for tuberculosis detection and drug-resistance profiling in resource-limited settings
items.summary: This paper developed a portable, field-ready nucleic acid diagnostic platform designed to detect tuberculosis and simultaneously profile drug resistance within a single unified system. The platform combines loop-mediated isothermal amplification targeting dual Mycobacterium tuberculosis genetic markers with an AI-enabled compact device and smartphone-based readout, enabling point-of-care use without laboratory infrastructure. Clinical evaluation demonstrated high sensitivity and specificity, and real-world deployment in a primary healthcare setting by minimally trained personnel achieved sensitivity and specificity benchmarked against GeneXpert. Importantly, the same platform architecture was extended without modification to identify mutations associated with rifampicin and isoniazid resistance using a probe-free, allele-specific amplification approach. The authors conclude that by integrating robust molecular diagnostics with AI-driven automation in an accessible format, the system has strong potential to advance tuberculosis screening and drug-resistance profiling in high-burden, resource-limited settings.
items.citation: Nag S, Banerjee S, Banerjee S et al.. Field-ready portable rapid nucleic acid test for tuberculosis detection and drug-resistance profiling in resource-limited settings. 2026

items.title: A smear test aiding with an automated slide reader acquires equivalent sensitivity to MGIT960 culture in pulmonary tuberculosis diagnosis.
items.summary: This study evaluated whether an artificial intelligence-aided automated slide reader could improve the diagnostic sensitivity of sputum smear microscopy for pulmonary tuberculosis, addressing ongoing debate about the continued relevance of smear testing in the era of molecular diagnostics. Sputum samples from suspected tuberculosis patients were prepared with both Ziehl-Neelsen and auramine O staining, then scanned by the automated system under varying field-of-view settings, with manual fluorescence microscopy, liquid culture, and a molecular assay serving as reference standards. Among clinically confirmed tuberculosis patients, the automated auramine O method scanning a larger number of fields achieved a detection rate comparable to liquid culture, while all methods maintained very high specificity in patients with non-mycobacterial pulmonary disease. Expanding the number of fields examined substantially increased detection rates for both staining methods, with auramine O consistently outperforming Ziehl-Neelsen staining. The authors conclude that AI-aided auramine O smear microscopy offers a rapid, same-day, and cost-effective diagnostic approach with sensitivity rivalling culture-based methods, making it a practical option for both high-throughput and resource-limited settings.
items.citation: Zhang C, Wang H, Chen Y et al.. A smear test aiding with an automated slide reader acquires equivalent sensitivity to MGIT960 culture in pulmonary tuberculosis diagnosis.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: Computer-aided detection thresholds can guide repeat rapid molecular testing in TB screening.
items.citation: Datta B, Story A, Rogers E et al.. Computer-aided detection thresholds can guide repeat rapid molecular testing in TB screening.. IJTLD open. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the relationship between lipid metabolism and cellular homeostasis, with particular emphasis on how lipid metabolic processes govern immune cell function across both innate and adaptive immunity. The authors synthesize the historical development of lipid metabolism research alongside current understanding of lipid transport, signaling pathways, and the roles of specific lipid metabolites in the proliferation, differentiation, and activation of macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils. The review further explores multilevel regulatory mechanisms — spanning genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational levels — that shape immune cell behavior through lipid metabolism. Dysregulation of these processes is discussed in the context of diverse diseases including cancer, cardiovascular disease, autoimmune conditions, neurodegenerative disorders, and metabolic diseases. The authors conclude by surveying therapeutic strategies that target lipid metabolism, ongoing clinical efforts, and emerging opportunities involving lipid-derived biomarkers and novel interventions.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Insulin Resistance and Inflammation
items.summary: This paper examined the bidirectional relationship between insulin resistance and inflammation, with a particular focus on how each condition drives and perpetuates the other in the context of cardiometabolic and vascular disease. The authors conducted a conceptual narrative review, synthesizing evidence across multiple levels of biological organization — from insulin receptor signaling and intracellular stress pathways to tissue dysfunction and systemic inflammatory amplification — while carefully distinguishing mechanistic and interventional evidence from observational associations. They argue that chronic nutrient excess activates overlapping stress programs, including oxidative, endoplasmic reticulum, inflammatory, and fibrotic pathways, that become maladaptive and self-reinforcing rather than protective. The central proposal is an integrative framework in which insulin resistance and meta-inflammation form mutually amplifying loops that accelerate endothelial dysfunction and atherogenesis. The authors suggest this framework can help prioritize mechanistic research and inform translational hypothesis testing aimed at interrupting these convergent pathological cycles.
items.citation: Evgenii Gusev, Alexey Sarapultsev, Yulia A. Zhuravleva. Insulin Resistance and Inflammation. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Sepsis
items.citation: Mervyn Singer, Derek C Angus, Djillali Annane et al.. Sepsis. The Lancet. 2026

items.title: Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers
items.summary: This review examines extracellular vesicles, which are small membrane-bound particles that carry lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids reflecting the state of their originating cells and act as key mediators of communication between cells. The authors synthesize current knowledge on how the major EV classes—including exosomes, microvesicles, apoptotic bodies, and oncosomes—are formed, isolated, and characterized using omics-based approaches. The review covers the roles of EVs in viral infection, inflammation, immune regulation, and cancer biology, as well as the therapeutic promise of mesenchymal stem cell-derived EVs in regenerative medicine and the modulation of EV signaling through photobiomodulation. The authors also address critical obstacles to clinical translation, including the need for standardized production, scalable manufacturing, and harmonized regulatory frameworks under bodies such as the FDA and EMA. Overall, the review positions EVs as potentially transformative tools for next-generation diagnostics and therapeutics in precision medicine, contingent on resolving outstanding technical and regulatory challenges.
items.citation: Dragan Primorac, Petar Brlek, Luka Bulić et al.. Therapeutic Potential of Extracellular Vesicles: From Biogenesis, Isolation and Molecular Characterization to Addressing Translational Gaps and Regulatory Barriers. International Journal of Molecular Sciences. 2026

items.title: Implementation of the NHS England Lung Cancer Screening Programme over 5 years
items.summary: The paper examined the implementation and outcomes of the NHS England Lung Cancer Screening Programme, a national initiative using low-dose computed tomography to detect lung cancer early in high-risk individuals. The programme targeted people aged 55–74 who had ever smoked and met risk thresholds determined by multivariable models, delivering screening through a regionally federated clinical infrastructure underpinned by national strategic and economic frameworks. Over five years, the programme invited more than two million people and diagnosed thousands of lung cancers, with a substantial majority detected at early tumor stages, representing an improvement in early-stage diagnosis proportions across England, particularly in socioeconomically deprived areas. The authors conclude that large-scale lung cancer screening is feasible and scalable, especially in reaching high-risk and underserved populations, though inequalities in participation remain an ongoing challenge to address. These findings are presented as evidence supporting broader adoption of lung cancer screening across the UK and internationally, alongside practical tools for adapting the programme to other healthcare contexts.
items.citation: Richard W. Lee, Arjun Nair, Haval Balata et al.. Implementation of the NHS England Lung Cancer Screening Programme over 5 years. Nature Medicine. 2026

items.title: Will AI Replace Physicians in the Near Future? AI Adoption Barriers in Medicine
items.summary: This paper examined whether current artificial intelligence technologies, specifically convolutional neural networks for medical imaging and large language models for clinical communication, are capable of replacing physicians in the near future. The authors conducted a narrative review of scientific literature addressing AI performance across medical imaging interpretation, clinical knowledge assessment, patient communication, and the technical, regulatory, and legal constraints surrounding AI deployment in healthcare. They found that while AI systems achieve high accuracy and reproducibility in narrowly defined tasks and can reduce interobserver variability and support workflow efficiency, significant barriers to physician replacement remain. These barriers include poor generalization beyond training data, inability to perform physical examinations or procedural tasks, susceptibility to hallucinations and overconfidence in language models, and unresolved questions of legal liability and accountability. The authors conclude that AI will augment rather than replace physicians, with the most plausible near-term trajectory being supervised automation of well-defined tasks while core clinical, ethical, and procedural responsibilities remain human-dependent.
items.citation: Rafał Obuchowicz, Adam Piórkowski, Karolina Nurzyńska et al.. Will AI Replace Physicians in the Near Future? AI Adoption Barriers in Medicine. Diagnostics. 2026

items.title: Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs
items.summary: This paper addresses the problem of detecting unsafe outputs from large language models during deployment, when alignment training alone is insufficient to guarantee safety. The authors propose a straightforward real-time monitoring approach that converts a safety verification signal from an external model into an alarm by applying a threshold calibrated through risk control. They evaluated this design on mathematical reasoning and adversarial red teaming datasets, comparing it against more sophisticated sequential hypothesis testing approaches. Their key finding is that this simple thresholding method performs competitively with the more complex alternatives, suggesting that elaborate monitoring frameworks may not be necessary for effective online safety oversight.
items.citation: Mona Schirmer, Metod Jazbec, Alexander Timans et al.. Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation
items.summary: This paper investigates a problem in on-policy self-distillation training of large language models, where a single model serves as both teacher and student with different levels of information access. The core issue identified is that dense token-level supervision from the teacher can cause privileged information leakage, where the student learns shortcuts dependent on answer information unavailable at test time, while also suppressing exploration and hurting generalization. To address this, the authors propose DemoPSD, a framework that selectively adopts teacher guidance by steering the student toward a reverse-KL barycenter target—a weighted geometric combination of teacher and student distributions—with the blending at each token position controlled adaptively based on the discrepancy between the two distributions. The framework is theoretically shown to attenuate privileged information leakage and preserve exploration capacity under dense distillation. Experiments across multiple scientific domains demonstrate that DemoPSD outperforms competing methods while maintaining higher training entropy and generalizing better to out-of-distribution benchmarks.
items.citation: Yunhe Li, Hao Shi, Wenhao Liu et al.. DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of building traffic simulation agents that both realistically imitate logged driving behavior and can be deliberately steered along interpretable behavioral dimensions. The authors developed Controllable Neural Variational Agents, a framework that infers per-agent behavior latents from discounted returns using a closed-form variational update, combined with a rectified-flow trajectory generator trained with classifier-free guidance through a mixed channel-mask curriculum. To address sparse reward signals, they introduced soft eligibility gates using smooth exponential decay rather than hard binary thresholds, preserving gradient information for agents near reward boundaries. Evaluated on a large-scale open motion dataset, the framework achieves competitive realism while enabling per-channel controllability over speed, acceleration, safety, and map compliance that purely imitative models lack. The work also highlights that steering metrics must be interpreted alongside physical plausibility constraints to avoid reward-hacking artifacts, where apparent behavioral improvements mask physically implausible trajectories.
items.citation: Juanwu Lu, Junyu Zhu, Ziran Wang. Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: The Debris Disk Host $β$ Piscis Austrinus is a Rapidly Rotating Star Seen Nearly Pole-On
items.summary: This paper investigated the physical properties of β Piscis Austrinus, a debris disk host star whose unusual spectral line shapes had previously suggested it might be a rapidly rotating star viewed nearly pole-on. Using a spectral synthesis code to model high-resolution spectra, the researchers determined the star's inclination, rotational rate, and photospheric properties, with additional temperature constraints from iron line ratios. The analysis confirmed that the star is oriented almost directly pole-on and rotates at a rate close to its critical velocity, producing significant gravity darkening such that its polar temperature is substantially hotter than its equatorial temperature and its apparent luminosity is nearly half again larger than its true luminosity. The same technique applied to Vega reproduced results consistent with interferometric measurements, validating the methodology. Comparison with stellar evolutionary models yielded a mass and an age for β PsA that agrees with an independent age estimate from its gravitationally associated companion star, demonstrating that spectroscopy alone can characterize rapidly rotating stars in this orientation.
items.citation: Colin Kane, Russel White, Jeremy Jones et al.. The Debris Disk Host $β$ Piscis Austrinus is a Rapidly Rotating Star Seen Nearly Pole-On. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Cryogenic RF characterization of the MAGO cavity for high-frequency gravitational-wave detection
items.summary: This paper investigated the use of superconducting radio-frequency cavities as resonant sensors for detecting gravitational waves in the kilohertz-to-megahertz frequency range. The researchers performed cryogenic radio-frequency characterization of a prototype superconducting niobium cavity with an unconventional geometry specifically designed to achieve narrow electromagnetic mode separation. Testing was conducted at two facilities at temperatures down to two kelvin, following an adapted surface preparation procedure, and included measurements of electromagnetic quality factors, mechanical tuning, phase transfer characteristics, and mechanical eigenmodes. The cavity achieved a small mode splitting through mechanical tuning at room temperature, high electromagnetic quality factors consistent with earlier prototypes, and phase transfer properties suitable for stable RF control, though mode coupling potentially attributable to one-point multipacting was also observed. Notably, the measured mechanical quality factors fell significantly below commonly assumed theoretical values, and the results overall demonstrate that established superconducting radio-frequency preparation and characterization techniques can be successfully applied to non-standard resonator geometries, providing experimental guidance for future gravitational-wave detector development.
items.citation: Can Dokuyucu, Bianca Giaccone, Tom Krokotsch et al.. Cryogenic RF characterization of the MAGO cavity for high-frequency gravitational-wave detection. arXiv. 2026

items.title: TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of evaluating whether AI agents can correctly co-evolve software tests alongside code changes, a task that existing benchmarks handle poorly by isolating tests from code changes and failing to verify executability. The authors introduce TestEvo-Bench, a benchmark mined from real software repository commit histories that encompasses two tracks—test generation and test update—each packaged with execution environments to support metrics such as pass rate, code coverage, and mutation score. The benchmark is designed as a "live" system, periodically ingesting new tasks and recording timestamps so that evaluations can be restricted to tasks postdating a model's training cutoff, reducing data leakage. Experiments with several state-of-the-art agents combining strong scaffolding frameworks with leading foundation models show competitive success rates on the overall benchmark, but performance drops notably on the most recent tasks and under constrained computational budgets, suggesting that current agents have not fully learned to generalize co-evolution reasoning. The findings highlight that execution-grounded, temporally controlled evaluation reveals limitations in test automation agents that static benchmarks would obscure.
items.citation: Jiale Amber Wang, Kaiyuan Wang, Pengyu Nie. TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Cost-effectiveness of a decentralised molecular point-of-care testing programme for sexually transmitted infections in remote primary care health services in Australia.
items.summary: This study examined whether a decentralised molecular point-of-care testing programme for sexually transmitted infections is cost-effective compared with standard laboratory-based testing in remote Australian First Nations communities, where STI burden is disproportionately high among young people. Researchers built a decision analytic model comparing costs and health outcomes over a ten-year horizon for individuals aged sixteen to twenty-nine years seeking care, using a government payer perspective with cost per quality-adjusted life year as the primary outcome measure. The model drew on data from a long-running community-led programme that had already demonstrated improvements in treatment uptake and timeliness for chlamydia, gonorrhoea, and trichomonas infections following a randomised trial. The analysis found that the point-of-care programme was cost-effective relative to the accepted willingness-to-pay threshold, with the result most sensitive to the probability of infection and receiving treatment within two days, and the programme was predicted to substantially reduce rates of pelvic inflammatory disease and preterm or low birthweight births. The authors conclude that sustained, scaled point-of-care testing in remote primary care settings represents good value and is likely to improve reproductive health outcomes for this population.
items.citation: Watts CG, Causer LM, Matthews SJ et al.. Cost-effectiveness of a decentralised molecular point-of-care testing programme for sexually transmitted infections in remote primary care health services in Australia.. Sexually transmitted infections. 2026

items.title: Use of point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in community outreach settings in Dallas, Texas, USA.
items.summary: This study examined the implementation of rapid point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in non-clinical, community outreach settings in an urban environment. Researchers conducted a program evaluation and retrospective data review of individuals tested at these sites, using statistical tests to explore whether demographic characteristics or symptom presence were associated with testing positive. The testing platform delivered results within thirty minutes, and all individuals diagnosed with either infection were referred for treatment within a single day. No statistically significant associations were found between positive test results and demographics or symptoms, though the small sample size limited the strength of these analyses. The authors conclude that point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing is feasible for rapid diagnosis and same-day treatment referral in outreach settings, while calling for further research into scalability, cost, and treatment completion rates.
items.citation: Blanchard HL, Gonzalez E, Chow J. Use of point-of-care nucleic acid amplification testing for chlamydia and gonorrhea in community outreach settings in Dallas, Texas, USA.. Sexual health. 2026

items.title: From syndromic management to diagnostic-led management: a report from an expert meeting on introducing sexually transmitted infection testing in low-resource settings.
items.summary: This paper examined the challenges and opportunities involved in transitioning from syndromic management to diagnostic-led approaches for sexually transmitted infections in low-resource settings, with a focus on sub-Saharan Africa. An expert meeting brought together specialists across STI diagnostics, implementation research, policy, regulatory affairs, and clinical practice to identify priorities and strategies for introducing and scaling up point-of-care testing for key STIs including chlamydia, gonorrhea, and trichomoniasis. The experts mapped barriers and opportunities at four levels — individual client, health service delivery, national, and global — and generated recommendations at each level. These recommendations included improving public knowledge of STIs, optimizing clinical workflows, expanding testing beyond traditional facilities, updating national treatment guidelines, strengthening laboratory quality assurance, harmonizing regulatory requirements across regions, and expanding manufacturing capacity through technology transfer.
