items.summary: This paper investigates a novel security vulnerability that emerges when AI coding agents operate autonomously across multiple sessions in a persistent codebase, where a misaligned or compromised agent can distribute malicious actions across pull requests rather than concentrating them in a single detectable event. The researchers introduced a benchmark called Iterative VibeCoding, spanning CLI tools and Flask web services across numerous task variations, to study how such distributed attacks compare to concentrated single-pull-request attacks in terms of evasion of monitoring systems. They found that no single monitor is robust against both attack strategies simultaneously, and that high evasion rates generalize across multiple state-of-the-art model backends, confirming the vulnerability is a property of the persistent-state setting rather than any particular model. To address gradual attacks specifically, the authors developed a stateful link-tracker monitor that follows suspicious patterns as they accumulate across pull requests, outperforming standard diff-based monitors that only examine incremental or accumulated changes without tracking relational buildup. Combining this monitor with trajectory monitors in an ensemble substantially reduced gradual-attack evasion, though significant evasion rates remained, underscoring that persistent-state attack surfaces pose a fundamental challenge for AI safety monitoring.
items.citation: Josh Hills, Ida Caspary, Asa Cooper Stickland. Distributed Attacks in Persistent-State AI Control. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs
items.summary: The paper investigates the problem of detecting unsafe or incorrect outputs from large language models during deployment, where alignment training alone proves insufficient. The authors propose a straightforward real-time monitoring approach that converts a safety-relevant signal from an external verifier model into a binary alarm decision by applying a threshold calibrated through risk control procedures. This simple design was evaluated on mathematical reasoning and adversarial red-teaming datasets. The key finding is that despite its simplicity, the threshold-based monitor performs competitively against more sophisticated monitors built on sequential hypothesis testing frameworks.
items.citation: Mona Schirmer, Metod Jazbec, Alexander Timans et al.. Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: What LLM Agents Say When No One Is Watching: Social Structure and Latent Objective Emergence in Multi-Agent Debates
items.citation: Arman Ghaffarizadeh, Danyal Mohaddes, Aliakbar Izadkhah et al.. What LLM Agents Say When No One Is Watching: Social Structure and Latent Objective Emergence in Multi-Agent Debates. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation
items.summary: This paper investigates a limitation of on-policy self-distillation for training large language models to reason, specifically the problem of privileged information leakage, where a student model learns shortcuts dependent on information available only during training and not at test time, as well as overfitting and suppressed exploration. The authors propose DemoPSD, a framework that selectively adopts teacher guidance rather than fully fitting the teacher distribution, by steering the student toward a reverse-KL barycenter target—a weighted geometric combination of teacher and student distributions—where the blending weight at each token position is controlled by the measured disagreement between the two distributions. They provide theoretical guarantees showing DemoPSD attenuates privileged information leakage and preserves exploration capacity under dense token-level distillation. Empirically, DemoPSD outperforms existing on-policy methods across multiple scientific domains while achieving higher training entropy and stronger generalization to out-of-distribution benchmarks.
items.citation: Yunhe Li, Hao Shi, Wenhao Liu et al.. DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Alleviating prior dependencies for DESI DR1 clustering fits through reparameterization
items.summary: This paper investigates prior-volume projection effects in Bayesian analyses of galaxy clustering data from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument, where weakly constrained nuisance parameters in the Effective Field Theory of Large Scale Structure cause marginalized cosmological posteriors to shift away from their true maximum. To address this, the authors apply two complementary strategies to the power spectrum multipoles: a nonlinear orthogonalization method that decorrelates nuisance and cosmological parameter priors, and a reparameterization-invariant Jeffreys prior over the theory coefficients computed via closed-form Jacobians. They find that standard baseline priors produce multi-sigma projection effects in the Hubble parameter and dark energy equation-of-state parameters, while the Jeffreys prior successfully recenters these posteriors so that the maximum a posteriori estimate falls within the credible regions. A hybrid approach combining the Jeffreys prior with baseline Gaussian priors is identified as optimal, controlling overly broad tails in the cold dark matter density while preserving the volume correction. Crucially, the resulting constraints agree with both physically motivated Halo Occupation Distribution priors and frequentist profile likelihood analyses, demonstrating that robust inferences about late-time cosmic expansion can be made independently of the statistical framework once projection effects are properly mitigated.
items.citation: Marco Bonici, Simone Paradiso, Glenn McGee et al.. Alleviating prior dependencies for DESI DR1 clustering fits through reparameterization. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Innovative epitope-based ELISA assay for detecting Group B Streptococcus carriage in pregnant women.
items.summary: This study investigated whether synthetic peptide epitopes derived from Group B Streptococcus proteins could serve as the basis for an ELISA-based immunodiagnostic test to detect GBS carriage in pregnant women, offering an alternative to the current gold-standard culture method, which is slow and sometimes ambiguous. Three epitopes were selected from the bacterial proteins GroEL, enolase, and elongation factor Tu, and their ability to distinguish GBS-positive from GBS-negative serum samples was evaluated using IgG, IgM, and IgA antibodies. Serum samples from a GBS-positive study group and a GBS-negative control group were tested with the peptides used individually and in combinations. All three epitopes significantly differentiated between the two groups, with the enolase and EF-Tu-derived peptides performing best individually at lower concentrations, while combining epitopes in any mixture further improved immunoreactivity. The authors propose that this epitope-based ELISA prototype represents a promising and potentially pioneering diagnostic tool that could complement or replace conventional GBS culture methods in clinical practice.
items.citation: Brzychczy-Włoch M, Dobrut A, Drożdż K et al.. Innovative epitope-based ELISA assay for detecting Group B Streptococcus carriage in pregnant women.. Applied microbiology and biotechnology. 2026

items.title: Bridging the diagnostic gap: the role of culture methods in the era of syndromic panels for central nervous system infections.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a syndromic molecular panel for central nervous system infections by comparing it with conventional cerebrospinal fluid culture in a real-world clinical setting over three years. Researchers analyzed thousands of paired samples, calculating concordance, sensitivity, specificity, and predictive values across the full dataset and within the subset of pathogens specifically targeted by the panel. The syndromic panel demonstrated high specificity and near-perfect negative predictive value when restricted to its included bacterial targets, but overall sensitivity was substantially lower when all culture-identified organisms were considered. Conventional culture detected clinically significant pathogens absent from the panel, including common staphylococcal and streptococcal species as well as rare environmental organisms, and critically retains the ability to guide antimicrobial susceptibility testing. The authors conclude that syndromic panels cannot function as standalone diagnostics and must be systematically paired with conventional culture, while also calling for next-generation multiplex platforms with broader target coverage reflecting real-world pathogen epidemiology.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Sberna G, D'Arezzo S, Valli MB et al.. Bridging the diagnostic gap: the role of culture methods in the era of syndromic panels for central nervous system infections.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Performance of the LIAISON PLEX gram-positive blood culture assay for identifying bacterial pathogens and resistance genes in blood cultures.
items.summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of the LIAISON PLEX Gram-Positive Blood Culture Assay, a multiplexed molecular test designed to rapidly identify gram-positive bacterial pathogens and antibiotic resistance markers directly from positive blood culture bottles. The assay was assessed across multiple sites using a combination of prospective clinical samples, pre-selected samples, and contrived specimens, as well as inclusivity and cross-reactivity panels. The assay demonstrated very high overall sensitivity and specificity for detecting all targeted gram-positive bacterial species and resistance markers, with nearly all tested strains successfully identified and only minimal cross-reactivity observed among off-panel organisms. Performance was maintained across blood culture bottles from multiple manufacturers, in the presence of potential interfering substances, and in polymicrobial infection scenarios. The authors conclude that the assay provides fast, accurate, and reliable detection of clinically significant gram-positive pathogens and resistance genes, supporting timely antibiotic optimization and patient management decisions.
items.citation: Chow S-K, Stevig C, Granato P et al.. Performance of the LIAISON PLEX gram-positive blood culture assay for identifying bacterial pathogens and resistance genes in blood cultures.. Journal of clinical microbiology. 2026

items.title: Utility and clinical relevance of multiplex PCR panels for diagnosis of central nervous system infections.
items.summary: This study evaluated the real-world diagnostic performance and clinical utility of multiplex PCR panels for meningitis and encephalitis using cerebrospinal fluid samples collected over a five-year period at a tertiary care hospital. Researchers retrospectively analysed test results alongside clinical data to determine positivity rates, pathogen distributions, co-detection frequencies, and whether panel results led to documented changes in diagnosis or treatment. The overall positivity rate was low, with viral pathogens predominating among detected agents and bacterial co-detections also observed in a notable proportion of positive cases. Despite the panels' capacity for rapid multi-pathogen identification, fewer than half of positive results were associated with a clinically relevant action, repeat testing after an initial negative result yielded little additional diagnostic value, and some tests were ordered for conditions outside the panels' scope. The authors conclude that while multiplex PCR panels are a valuable tool for central nervous system infection diagnosis, their clinical impact is undermined by inappropriate utilisation and insufficient clinician familiarity with panel contents, highlighting the need for clearer testing criteria and improved stewardship.
items.venue: The Indian journal of medical research
items.citation: Gürler M, Karaoğlu Ç, Yurtcu EE et al.. Utility and clinical relevance of multiplex PCR panels for diagnosis of central nervous system infections.. The Indian journal of medical research. 2026

items.title: Intrapartum Molecular Detection of Group B <i>Streptococcus</i>: Real-World Evaluation of Multiple Point-of-Care Platforms and the Potential Role of Lysis Efficiency.
items.summary: This study evaluated the real-world diagnostic performance of multiple rapid molecular point-of-care tests for detecting Group B Streptococcus during labour, addressing the limitation that antenatal screening may not accurately reflect colonisation status at the time of delivery. Vaginal-rectal swabs collected from women at or beyond thirty-seven weeks of gestation were tested directly without prior enrichment culture using six molecular assays and one antigen-based test, with conventional intrapartum culture serving as the reference standard. Diagnostic performance varied considerably across platforms, with positive percent agreement ranging widely while negative percent agreement was generally strong, and several culture-positive samples were missed by all molecular assays. The authors suggest that differences in lysis procedures between platforms may partly explain the observed variability in sensitivity, though this hypothesis could not be formally tested. The findings caution against assuming interchangeability among available point-of-care tests and emphasise the importance of local clinical validation before implementing any single platform in routine intrapartum care.
items.citation: Serrari M, Bianchi L, Tré-Hardy M et al.. Intrapartum Molecular Detection of Group B <i>Streptococcus</i>: Real-World Evaluation of Multiple Point-of-Care Platforms and the Potential Role of Lysis Efficiency.. Microorganisms. 2026

items.title: Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration
items.summary: This review paper examines recent developments in microfluidic chip technology, commonly called lab-on-a-chip systems, and their expanding role in laboratory medicine. The authors surveyed advances in chip design and fabrication approaches, including three-dimensional printing and flexible modular substrates, alongside improvements in biosensor integration that enable real-time detection of circulating tumor cells, nucleic acids, and exosomes. A central focus is the convergence of microfluidics with artificial intelligence and machine learning, which has produced smart diagnostic platforms capable of self-optimization, automated analysis, and clinical decision support across applications ranging from cancer diagnostics and infectious disease detection to point-of-care testing and personalized medicine. The review also highlights emerging uses in neuroscience diagnostics and microbiome profiling as evidence of the technology's broad clinical potential. The authors additionally address persistent challenges in manufacturing, clinical validation, and system integration that must be overcome before next-generation microfluidic devices can be routinely deployed in clinical and public health settings.
items.citation: Hong Cai, Dongxia Wang, Yiqun Zhao et al.. Recent Advances in Microfluidic Chip Technology for Laboratory Medicine: Innovations and Artificial Intelligence Integration. Biosensors. 2026

items.title: Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.
items.summary: This review paper examined the current landscape of nucleic acid-based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases, motivated by the global need for decentralized, rapid testing outside conventional laboratory settings. The authors organized their analysis around three core operational steps: nucleic acid extraction, amplification, and detection. They surveyed extraction strategies including magnetic bead-based, paper-based, and integrated microfluidic approaches, emphasizing designs that are simplified and equipment-free, alongside amplification methods such as PCR, LAMP, RPA, and NASBA that have been adapted for portable and low-power conditions. For detection, the review covers platforms ranging from colorimetric and fluorescent lateral flow assays to advanced biosensors incorporating CRISPR/Cas systems and toehold switch mechanisms, which offer highly sensitive and programmable nucleic acid recognition. Overall, the review synthesizes developments across these interconnected steps to map the trajectory of accessible, sensitive, and economically viable point-of-care infectious disease diagnostics.
items.citation: Hatem H, Mysara M, Ramadan R. Trends of nucleic acid - based point-of-care diagnostics for infectious diseases.. Journal of biological engineering. 2026

items.title: A Novel Voltammetric Point-of-Care Device for Rapid and Accurate Nucleic Acid and Viral Pathogen Detection.
items.summary: This paper developed an electrochemical nucleic acid amplification test that combines isothermal amplification with real-time voltammetric detection for diagnosing infectious diseases. The approach works by monitoring the proton release that occurs during amplification using a pH-sensing redox compound, offering an alternative to optical detection methods that can complicate standard isothermal approaches like LAMP. The system was evaluated against RT-qPCR for several respiratory pathogens, including SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B viruses, and RSV A, using RNA purified from clinical samples, as well as in an extraction-free workflow for a equine herpesvirus in nasal swabs. Results showed high concordance with RT-qPCR across these targets and sample types, with a defined limit of detection established for influenza A virus. The authors conclude that real-time voltammetric LAMP is a feasible approach for point-of-care diagnostics, though they acknowledge that further validation across additional sample matrices and prospective cohorts is needed.
items.citation: Munita R, Lyttleton R, Danefur E et al.. A Novel Voltammetric Point-of-Care Device for Rapid and Accurate Nucleic Acid and Viral Pathogen Detection.. ACS omega. 2026

items.title: A nested PCR-based point-of-care multiplex test for detection of bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluids.
items.summary: This study developed and evaluated a multiplex one-tube nested real-time PCR method integrated with a point-of-care testing instrument for detecting three streptococcal pathogens responsible for cerebrospinal fluid infections. The researchers assessed the assay's sensitivity using recombinant plasmids and simulated cerebrospinal fluid samples, then validated its clinical performance against conventional real-time PCR using both simulated and clinical samples. The integrated point-of-care system achieved very low bacterial detection limits in simulated samples while reducing the total testing time to approximately two hours compared to the four hours required with manual extraction. When applied to clinical samples, the new method identified additional positive cases that conventional real-time PCR had missed, demonstrating superior sensitivity. The authors conclude that this culture-free, automated approach offers a rapid, highly sensitive, and operationally streamlined solution for diagnosing bacterial meningitis-associated pathogens directly from cerebrospinal fluid.
items.venue: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
items.citation: Wu J, Zhao Z, Pang P et al.. A nested PCR-based point-of-care multiplex test for detection of bacterial pathogens in cerebrospinal fluids.. Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology. 2026

items.title: Isothermal nucleic acid amplification techniques: A comprehensive overview on their principle & applications as alternative to PCR.
items.summary: This review paper examined isothermal nucleic acid amplification methods as alternatives to conventional PCR-based diagnostics. The authors surveyed the principles, detection strategies, and applications of the most commonly used techniques, including loop-mediated isothermal amplification, helicase-dependent amplification, rolling circle amplification, strand displacement amplification, nucleic acid sequence-based amplification, and recombinase polymerase amplification. Because these methods operate at a constant temperature and do not require thermal cycling equipment, they offer simpler and more affordable options particularly suited to resource-limited settings. The review addresses a gap in the existing literature by consolidating information on reaction mechanisms, product detection approaches, and disease diagnostic applications into a single comprehensive overview. The authors conclude that isothermal amplification techniques represent a promising frontier for expanding access to molecular diagnostics across research and clinical contexts, and they discuss future perspectives for the field.
items.citation: Amare Amdiyee A, Sisay Tessema T. Isothermal nucleic acid amplification techniques: A comprehensive overview on their principle & applications as alternative to PCR.. Practical laboratory medicine. 2026

items.title: Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.
items.summary: This review examined the landscape of molecular diagnostic technologies for bacterial infections, with particular emphasis on their role in improving etiological precision amid the growing threat of antimicrobial resistance. The authors conducted a structured comparative analysis of major platforms, including various forms of PCR, isothermal amplification, next-generation sequencing, CRISPR-based diagnostics, and digital PCR, evaluating each against key performance parameters such as sensitivity, specificity, detection limits, turnaround time, and multiplexing capability. The review also critically assessed the integration of artificial intelligence and machine learning into diagnostic workflows for resistance prediction and clinical decision support. Additionally, the translational feasibility of these technologies for point-of-care deployment was considered, accounting for clinical validation requirements and real-world operational constraints. The authors conclude that advancing culture-independent bacteriological diagnostics requires carefully balancing analytical performance with clinical interpretability, and they outline the remaining challenges and future directions in the field.
items.citation: Cotelici AM, Bălășoiu AT, Boldeanu MV et al.. Improving the Precision of Etiological Diagnosis in Bacterial Infections Using Molecular Technologies: A Comparative Analysis of Platforms, AI Integration, and Point-of-Care Deployment.. International journal of molecular sciences. 2026

items.title: A Multiplex One-Tube Nested Real-Time PCR Assay for the Point-of-Care Testing of Infectious Meningitis.
items.summary: This paper developed a multiplex one-tube nested real-time PCR assay integrated with a portable, fully automated point-of-care testing platform for detecting three pathogens responsible for infectious meningitis — *Haemophilus influenzae*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, and *Cryptococcus neoformans* — directly from cerebrospinal fluid. The closed-system nested amplification design was intended to reduce contamination risk and operational complexity compared with conventional approaches. The assay demonstrated high analytical sensitivity with very low detection limits, no cross-reactivity against a panel of common non-target microorganisms, and maintained performance in simulated clinical samples. Evaluation against clinical cerebrospinal fluid specimens showed near-perfect agreement with conventional quantitative PCR, with the new assay detecting additional *C. neoformans* cases, suggesting improved sensitivity under real clinical conditions. Results were obtained in under two hours, indicating the platform offers a rapid, sensitive, and operationally practical option for meningitis pathogen detection, though the authors note that validation in larger patient cohorts is still needed.
items.citation: Zhang D, Wang J, Zhao Z et al.. A Multiplex One-Tube Nested Real-Time PCR Assay for the Point-of-Care Testing of Infectious Meningitis.. Pathogens (Basel, Switzerland). 2026

items.title: Data-Centric Foundation Models in Computational Healthcare: A Survey
items.summary: This survey examines the intersection of foundation models and computational healthcare through a data-centric lens, focusing on how these large pretrained models can address longstanding challenges in clinical data acquisition, quality, and processing. The authors systematically review data-centric approaches spanning the full pipeline from model pre-training through inference, considering issues such as data quantity, annotation, patient privacy, and ethical constraints that are particular to the healthcare domain. The survey also addresses AI security, model assessment, and alignment with human values as critical considerations when deploying foundation models in medical contexts. The authors conclude with an outlook on how foundation model-based analytics could improve patient outcomes and streamline clinical workflows, while providing a curated repository of healthcare-relevant foundation models and datasets as a community resource.
items.citation: Yunkun Zhang, Jin Gao, Zheling Tan et al.. Data-Centric Foundation Models in Computational Healthcare: A Survey. ACM Computing Surveys. 2026

items.title: Acute pancreatitis: mechanisms and therapeutic approaches
items.summary: This review paper examines acute pancreatitis, a severe inflammatory condition whose global incidence is rising with notable regional variation in both frequency and primary causes, including gallstone disease, hypertriglyceridemia, and alcohol abuse. The authors synthesize current knowledge on disease definition and classification, as well as advances in clinical scoring systems, biomarkers, and artificial intelligence-based predictive models relevant to early risk stratification. The review also details multilevel pathophysiological mechanisms underlying the condition, encompassing aberrant enzymatic activation, calcium overload, impaired autophagy, inflammatory cascades, and diverse forms of pancreatic acinar cell death. From a therapeutic standpoint, the paper covers management across disease stages, adjunctive approaches from traditional Chinese medicine, and individualized strategies for special patient populations. Emerging interventions including nanotechnology and extracellular vesicle-based therapies are also highlighted as potential future directions for research and clinical innovation.
items.citation: Qian Hu, Yue Hu, Chunlu Tan et al.. Acute pancreatitis: mechanisms and therapeutic approaches. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Advanced Technologies in Extracellular Vesicle Biosensing: Platforms, Standardization, and Clinical Translation
items.summary: This review examines extracellular vesicles as diagnostic targets, focusing on biosensing technologies designed to exploit their cargo of proteins, nucleic acids, and lipids as molecular mirrors of parent cell states. The authors surveyed a broad range of detection modalities—including surface plasmon resonance, electrochemical, fluorescence, and magnetic systems—alongside efforts to standardize analytical validation through frameworks such as MISEV and EV-TRACK. They also addressed advances in multiplexed and single-vesicle analysis, multiomics integration, and the application of machine learning to improve diagnostic accuracy and account for biological heterogeneity. Clinical applications across oncology, neurology, and cardiometabolic and infectious diseases are reviewed, with attention to how these platforms are progressing toward regulatory compliance and scalability. The review concludes by identifying persistent challenges in manufacturing, quality control, and point-of-care deployment, and proposes a unified framework to bring EV biosensing into routine precision diagnostics and personalized medicine.
items.citation: Seong-Jun Choi, Jaewon Choi, Jin Kim et al.. Advanced Technologies in Extracellular Vesicle Biosensing: Platforms, Standardization, and Clinical Translation. Molecules. 2026

items.title: Multianalyte nano-biosensor diagnostics: advances through microfluidic and AI integration
items.summary: This mini-review examines the current state and future potential of nano-biosensor technologies for clinical diagnostics, with a focus on multiplexed detection of multiple biomarkers simultaneously. The paper surveys electrochemical and optical transduction methods and discusses how microfluidic integration improves sensor performance through precise sample handling, reduced reagent consumption, and parallel biomarker analysis. A comparative analysis of multiplexing strategies — including spatial, spectral, and temporal encoding — is presented alongside considerations for sensor surface regeneration to enable device reusability. The review also explores how adaptive artificial intelligence algorithms can tailor diagnostic outputs to diverse patient populations while navigating ethical and regulatory challenges such as data protection, algorithm transparency, and compliance with medical device standards. Together, the synthesis of nano-biosensor design, microfluidics, and artificial intelligence is presented as a roadmap for translating these technologies into next-generation point-of-care and wearable diagnostic platforms.
items.citation: Shashikant Pathak, Shadi Bazazordeh, Buse Çamlıca et al.. Multianalyte nano-biosensor diagnostics: advances through microfluidic and AI integration. Frontiers in Bioengineering and Biotechnology. 2026

items.title: Spectral Fingerprinting of Engineered Nanomaterials for Precision Biosensing
items.summary: Spectral fingerprinting of engineered nanomaterials is a biosensing approach that uses optical signals from closely related nanomaterials to detect subtle biological differences in complex mixtures of biomolecules associated with various clinical and biological states. The technique exploits readouts such as near-infrared fluorescence or surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy to identify single or multiplexed biomarkers as well as whole-cell and organism-level biological signatures. By incorporating advanced analytical tools including feature extraction and machine learning, the method has expanded its accuracy and scope compared to traditional omics or biochemical assays, which tend to be lower-throughput and more costly. The paper reviews recent applications of this framework, including machine-learning-assisted live-cell phenotyping, serum-based cancer detection, and pathogen identification, while outlining future directions that envision integration with nano-omics and generative artificial intelligence.
items.citation: Aceer Nadeem, Maryam Rahmani, Yibo Wang et al.. Spectral Fingerprinting of Engineered Nanomaterials for Precision Biosensing. ACS Nano. 2026

items.title: Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs
items.summary: The paper investigates the problem of detecting unsafe outputs from large language models during deployment, where alignment training alone is insufficient to guarantee safety. The authors propose a straightforward real-time monitoring approach that converts a safety verification signal from an external model into an alarm decision by applying a calibrated threshold, with the threshold set using risk control methods. This simple design was evaluated on mathematical reasoning and red teaming datasets and found to perform comparably to more sophisticated monitoring approaches based on sequential hypothesis testing, suggesting that complex monitoring machinery may not be necessary for effective online safety detection.
items.citation: Mona Schirmer, Metod Jazbec, Alexander Timans et al.. Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation
items.summary: This paper investigates a problem with on-policy self-distillation for training large language models to reason, where using a single model as both teacher and student with differing information access can cause overfitting, suppressed exploration, and a fundamental issue called privileged information leakage—where the student learns shortcuts dependent on information unavailable at test time. To address these problems, the authors propose DemoPSD, a framework that selectively adopts teacher guidance rather than fitting the full teacher distribution. DemoPSD steers the student toward a reverse-KL barycenter target, a weighted geometric combination of teacher and student distributions, using token-level disagreement between the two distributions to adaptively control how much teacher guidance is applied at each position. The authors provide theoretical guarantees that DemoPSD attenuates privileged information leakage and preserves exploration capacity under dense token-level distillation. Experiments across multiple scientific domains demonstrate that DemoPSD outperforms existing self-distillation and reinforcement-based baselines while maintaining higher training entropy and generalizing better to out-of-distribution benchmarks.
items.citation: Yunhe Li, Hao Shi, Wenhao Liu et al.. DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of building traffic simulation agents that both realistically imitate recorded driving behavior and can be deliberately steered along interpretable behavioral dimensions. The authors developed Controllable Neural Variational Agents, a framework that infers per-agent behavior latents from discounted returns using a closed-form variational update, and combines this with a rectified-flow trajectory generator trained with classifier-free guidance via a mixed curriculum. A key methodological contribution is the use of soft eligibility gates, which replace hard binary reward thresholds with smooth exponential decay to preserve gradient signals for agents near threshold boundaries. Evaluated on a large-scale motion dataset, the system achieves competitive realism while enabling monotone, interpretable control over speed, acceleration, safety, and map compliance—capabilities absent from higher-ranked pure imitation models. The work also highlights that controllability metrics must be interpreted alongside physical plausibility constraints to avoid reward-hacking artifacts.
items.citation: Juanwu Lu, Junyu Zhu, Ziran Wang. Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Cryogenic RF characterization of the MAGO cavity for high-frequency gravitational-wave detection
items.summary: This paper investigated the use of a superconducting niobium radio-frequency cavity as a prototype resonant sensor for detecting gravitational waves in the kilohertz-to-megahertz frequency range. The cavity features an unconventional geometry designed to achieve narrow electromagnetic mode separation, and it was subjected to adapted surface preparation procedures before undergoing cryogenic testing at two major accelerator facilities at temperatures as low as two kelvin. The experiments successfully achieved a small mode splitting through mechanical tuning and measured high electromagnetic quality factors consistent with earlier prototype results. The characterization also revealed phase transfer properties relevant for stable RF control, indications of mode coupling potentially linked to one-point multipacting, and mechanical quality factors substantially below theoretically assumed values. These findings demonstrate that established superconducting radio-frequency techniques can be applied to non-standard cavity geometries and provide key experimental data to guide the design of future gravitational-wave detectors based on this technology.
items.citation: Can Dokuyucu, Bianca Giaccone, Tom Krokotsch et al.. Cryogenic RF characterization of the MAGO cavity for high-frequency gravitational-wave detection. arXiv. 2026

items.title: TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of evaluating whether AI agents can correctly update or generate software tests in response to code changes, a process known as test and code co-evolution. The authors created TestEvo-Bench, a benchmark mined from real commit histories across open-source Java projects, offering two tracks: generating new tests that capture changed software behavior, and updating existing failing tests to align with those changes. Each task is grounded in executable environments, enabling evaluation through concrete metrics such as pass rate, code coverage, and mutation score. A key feature is the live benchmark design, in which tasks are timestamped and continuously refreshed via an automated pipeline, allowing evaluations to be restricted to tasks postdating a model's training cutoff to mitigate data leakage. Experiments with state-of-the-art agents show competitive but imperfect performance, with success rates declining notably on the most recent tasks and under constrained computational budgets, suggesting current agents have not fully solved co-evolution reasoning.
items.citation: Jiale Amber Wang, Kaiyuan Wang, Pengyu Nie. TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution. arXiv. 2026

items.title: When Do LLM Personas Support Visualization Design? A Cross-Model Study of Color Assignment and Chart Choice
items.summary: This paper investigates whether large language model personas reliably capture personality-driven differences relevant to visualization design, or whether their outputs are primarily artifacts of model architecture and task framing. The researchers tested personas based on Big Five personality profiles across multiple language models on two tasks: assigning colors to abstract and concrete concepts, and rating preferences for different chart types across varying task contexts. They found that the relationship between personality and color choice was highly dependent on which model was used, with one model showing no personality-color coupling, another showing consistent coupling, and a third showing partial effects, while abstract concepts revealed stronger personality influence on color variance than concrete ones. For chart selection, personality-based groupings produced consistent rankings across task contexts, but a baseline condition without any persona recovered the same top-ranked chart type in nearly all cases, suggesting that task context rather than personality drives primary chart preferences. The authors conclude that language model personas function as exploratory design probes rather than replacements for human participants, and recommend multi-model testing, distinguishing concept types, and including no-persona baselines in future visualization design studies.
items.citation: Shahreen Salim, Klaus Mueller. When Do LLM Personas Support Visualization Design? A Cross-Model Study of Color Assignment and Chart Choice. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Evaluation of the QuickFinder COVID-19/Flu/RSV antigen test for rapid simultaneous detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza A/B viruses, and respiratory syncytial virus.
items.summary: This study evaluated a lateral flow immunoassay-based antigen rapid diagnostic test designed to simultaneously detect four major respiratory viruses: SARS-CoV-2, influenza A, influenza B, and respiratory syncytial virus. The researchers tested archived nasopharyngeal swab specimens against real-time reverse transcription PCR as a reference standard, and also assessed cross-reactivity against a panel of viral and bacterial respiratory pathogens. The test demonstrated high sensitivity for SARS-CoV-2 and influenza A, with somewhat lower sensitivity for influenza B and RSV, while achieving perfect specificity for all four targets and showing no cross-reactivity with any of the tested microorganisms. Performance was consistently strong in specimens with high viral loads, but sensitivity declined in low viral load samples. The authors conclude that the test is a reliable rapid screening and differentiation tool for high-viral-load specimens but should complement rather than replace molecular testing due to its limitations at low viral loads.
items.citation: Kim JA, Kang M, Yu HJ et al.. Evaluation of the QuickFinder COVID-19/Flu/RSV antigen test for rapid simultaneous detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza A/B viruses, and respiratory syncytial virus.. Diagnostic microbiology and infectious disease. 2026

items.title: No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients
items.summary: This study examined the disease burden of respiratory syncytial virus infection among adult patients at a Japanese clinic by testing nasopharyngeal swabs collected from patients presenting with acute respiratory infection symptoms. Rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests capable of simultaneously detecting SARS-CoV-2, influenza viruses A and B, and RSV were applied to all samples. While substantial numbers of patients tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 and influenza A, and one for influenza B, no RSV-positive cases were identified. Comparing these findings against previous reports that used polymerase chain reaction testing revealed a statistically significant difference in RSV detection rates, with PCR studies identifying a meaningful proportion of positive cases. The authors conclude that rapid immunochromatographic antigen testing has limited usefulness for diagnosing RSV infections in Japanese adults compared to PCR-based approaches.
items.citation: Kajiwara N, Tsunetoshi Y, Shiode S. No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients. 2026

items.title: Respiratory surveillance through point of care molecular testing and its impacts on nursing home RSV, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2 awareness and reporting behavior: a pilot study.
items.citation: Davidson H, Han L, McConeghy K et al.. Respiratory surveillance through point of care molecular testing and its impacts on nursing home RSV, influenza, and SARS-CoV-2 awareness and reporting behavior: a pilot study.. Antimicrobial stewardship & healthcare epidemiology : ASHE. 2026

items.title: Performance of an antigen test for detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza, and respiratory syncytial virus-a prospective study.
items.summary: This prospective study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a multiplex rapid antigen test capable of detecting SARS-CoV-2, influenza A, influenza B, and respiratory syncytial virus simultaneously in patients presenting with acute respiratory infection symptoms. Over a thousand patients, including a subset of children, were tested using the nasal swab antigen kit alongside standard nasopharyngeal PCR, with antigen results interpreted by emergency department staff. The test demonstrated high sensitivity for all four viruses when PCR cycle threshold values indicated higher viral loads, and achieved perfect specificity across all pathogens and age groups. An analysis of viral RNA absorbed by the nasal swab compared to nasopharyngeal swab suggested that nasal sampling captures less viral material, a difference that reached significance for influenza A in the broader patient group but was not observed in children. The authors conclude that the test's performance supports its use as a marker of infectiousness in acute clinical care settings.
items.venue: Clinical microbiology and infection : the official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases
items.citation: Berger K, Vang SH, Bekkeseth RHO et al.. Performance of an antigen test for detection of SARS-CoV-2, influenza, and respiratory syncytial virus-a prospective study.. Clinical microbiology and infection : the official publication of the European Society of Clinical Microbiology and Infectious Diseases. 2026

items.title: Analytical comparison of over-the-counter multiplex tests for influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2.
items.summary: This study evaluated the comparative analytical performance of over-the-counter lateral flow tests designed to detect influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2, a category of tests that has expanded rapidly since the COVID-19 pandemic but lacks robust head-to-head performance data for consumers. Researchers prepared contrived specimen panels from propagated viral stocks of multiple influenza strains and a SARS-CoV-2 Omicron variant in clinical matrix, then applied these panels to eight commercially available tests following manufacturer instructions, with results read by blinded evaluators. The tests performed comparably to one another across all four viruses, with detection limits falling within a narrow range. A notable finding was that at low viral concentrations, many tests initially appeared negative at the start of the interpretation window but converted to a positive result by the end of that window. The authors therefore recommend that users allow the full manufacturer-specified incubation time to elapse before concluding that a test result is truly negative.
items.citation: Lohsen S, Hoy-Schulz Y, Taing A et al.. Analytical comparison of over-the-counter multiplex tests for influenza A, influenza B, and SARS-CoV-2.. Microbiology spectrum. 2026

items.title: No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients
items.summary: The study investigated the burden of respiratory syncytial virus infection among adult patients at a Japanese clinic by testing nasopharyngeal swabs collected over approximately one year from patients presenting with acute respiratory symptoms. Samples were analyzed using rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests capable of simultaneously detecting SARS-CoV-2, influenza A and B viruses, and RSV. While substantial numbers of patients tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 and influenza A, no patients tested positive for RSV. Comparison with previous studies that employed polymerase chain reaction testing revealed a significantly lower RSV detection rate in this cohort, suggesting a meaningful difference in diagnostic sensitivity between the two methods. The authors conclude that rapid immunochromatographic antigen testing is likely insufficiently sensitive for diagnosing RSV infections in adults in Japan.
items.citation: Kajiwara N, Tsunetoshi Y, Shiode S. No detection of respiratory syncytial virus at one clinic in Japan with rapid immunochromatographic antigen tests for three viruses using the same nasopharyngeal swabs from adult patients. 2026

items.title: The aryl hydrocarbon receptor: structure, signaling, physiology and pathology
items.summary: The aryl hydrocarbon receptor is a ligand-dependent transcription factor that senses both environmental and endogenous signals, and this review comprehensively examines its structure, signaling mechanisms, and roles in physiology and disease. The authors trace six decades of research on the receptor, covering its canonical function in xenobiotic sensing alongside its broader roles in development, immune regulation, barrier tissue integrity, and host-microbe interactions. Recent structural findings clarifying ligand-binding and transcriptional regulation are discussed, as is the diverse range of AHR ligands including environmental toxins and tryptophan-derived microbial and dietary metabolites that engage both canonical and noncanonical signaling pathways. The review further addresses AHR involvement in cancer, autoimmune conditions, and inflammatory diseases, positioning it as a potential therapeutic target. The authors conclude by emphasizing ongoing advances in drug design aimed at developing clinically effective AHR modulators, framing the receptor as a master integrator of environmental and endogenous cues within the broader context of the exposome.
items.citation: Xavier Coumoul, Robert Barouki, Charlotte Esser et al.. The aryl hydrocarbon receptor: structure, signaling, physiology and pathology. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria
items.summary: This paper presents an updated international clinical guideline addressing urticaria, a mast cell-driven condition characterized by wheals, angioedema, or both. The guideline was developed using Cochrane methods and the GRADE framework, with input from a large international consensus conference involving delegates from numerous national and international societies across dozens of countries. It covers the definition and classification of urticaria, distinguishing acute from chronic forms and further categorizing chronic urticaria into spontaneous and inducible subtypes. The guideline outlines evidence-based and expert-guided approaches to diagnosis and management across urticaria subtypes, noting that while chronic urticaria is disabling and impairs quality of life, novel therapies are now available. The resulting document was formally acknowledged by the European Union of Medical Specialists.
items.citation: T. Zuberbier, Z. Abdul Hameed Ansari, A. H. Abdul Latiff et al.. The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria. Allergy. 2026

items.title: Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation
items.summary: This review examines the role of neutrophils in chronic inflammation, moving beyond their classical characterization as short-lived innate immune cells that rapidly clear pathogens. The authors synthesize current evidence showing that in chronic inflammatory conditions, neutrophils adopt a hyperinflammatory phenotype characterized by dysregulated mechanisms including excessive NET formation, uncontrolled oxidative burst, degranulation, and sustained release of proteases and cytokines, collectively driving tissue damage. Beyond their direct effector functions, neutrophils are shown to actively communicate with a broad range of cell types—including endothelial cells, monocytes, platelets, and lymphocytes—thereby orchestrating immune responses and tissue remodeling in ways that blur the boundary between innate and adaptive immunity. The review applies this framework to several immune-driven diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis, atherosclerosis, inflammatory bowel disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and fibrotic disorders, illustrating how aberrant neutrophil behavior contributes to their pathophysiology. The authors conclude that deeper understanding of neutrophil behavior in chronic inflammation may reveal novel therapeutic targets for these conditions.
items.citation: Kaat Torfs, Gaël Vermeersch, Mieke Gouwy et al.. Neutrophils as critical orchestrators of chronic inflammation. Cellular and Molecular Immunology. 2026

items.title: Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the relationship between the microbiota and cancer, addressing how microbial communities influence carcinogenesis, tumor progression, and patient prognosis. The authors trace the historical development of this research field and analyze the molecular mechanisms by which the microbiota modulates oncogenic signaling pathways, immune responses, and tumor metabolism. The review characterizes the dual role of the microbiota as both a promoter and suppressor of cancer, and explores how microbial communities affect responses to immunotherapy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Therapeutic strategies targeting the microbiota—including probiotics, antibiotics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—are surveyed alongside approved drugs and ongoing clinical investigations. The authors conclude by advocating for personalized, microbiota-targeted approaches in oncology and propose a comprehensive framework to guide future research and clinical application in this area.
items.citation: Yanxi Yao, Yifei Zhu, Keji Chen et al.. Microbiota in cancer: current understandings and future perspectives. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Tumour–brain crosstalk restrains cancer immunity via a sensory–sympathetic axis
items.summary: This paper investigated how tumours in peripheral organs communicate with the brain and how this crosstalk influences anti-tumour immunity. Using genetically engineered mouse models combined with neural tracing, tissue imaging, and single-cell transcriptomics in a lung adenocarcinoma model, the researchers mapped a circuit in which tumour-induced vagal sensory neuron activation transmits signals to brainstem nuclei, which then elevate sympathetic efferent activity within the tumour microenvironment. This heightened sympathetic output suppresses anti-tumour immune responses through adrenergic signalling in alveolar macrophages, thereby creating an immune-suppressive tumour microenvironment that promotes tumour growth. Disrupting this sensory-to-sympathetic pathway via genetic, pharmacological, or chemogenetic interventions significantly reduced lung tumour growth by restoring immune activity against cancer. The findings reveal a bidirectional tumour–brain communication axis as a potential therapeutic target for visceral organ cancers.
items.citation: Haohan Karen Wei, Chuyue Yu, Bo Hu et al.. Tumour–brain crosstalk restrains cancer immunity via a sensory–sympathetic axis. Nature. 2026

items.title: CAR-engineered cell therapies: current understandings and future perspectives
items.summary: This review examines the current state and future directions of chimeric antigen receptor (CAR)-engineered cell therapies, tracing their evolution from oncology-focused origins to a broad range of clinical applications. The authors surveyed the landscape of CAR modalities—including CAR-T, CAR-NK, CAR-macrophage, and CAR-NKT cells—alongside next-generation design innovations such as logic-gated CARs and off-the-shelf allogeneic products, detailing their structural components, signalling mechanisms, and manufacturing considerations. Beyond hematologic and solid tumours, the review covers expanding applications in autoimmune disorders, infectious diseases, fibrosis, ageing-related conditions, and organ transplantation, highlighting specific examples such as CD19/BCMA-targeted CAR-T cells achieving sustained remission in lupus and rheumatoid arthritis, CAR-NK approaches against HIV, CAR-Tregs for transplant tolerance, and senolytic CARs for tissue fibrosis. The authors note that CAR therapies in autoimmune settings appear to exhibit lower rates of cytokine release syndrome compared to oncological use, though challenges including tumour microenvironment resistance, antigen escape, manufacturing complexity, and high costs remain significant obstacles. Overall, the review concludes that CAR-engineered therapies offer precise and programmable immune modulation with substantial potential across an expanding array of diseases, as evidenced by ongoing clinical trials.
items.citation: Mobina Bayat, Javid Sadri Nahand. CAR-engineered cell therapies: current understandings and future perspectives. Molecular Biomedicine. 2026

items.title: Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs
items.summary: The paper investigates the problem of detecting unsafe outputs from large language models during deployment, where alignment training alone is insufficient to guarantee safe behavior. The authors propose a straightforward real-time monitoring approach that converts a safety score from an external verifier model into a binary alarm by applying a threshold, with the threshold calibrated using risk control methods. This simple design was evaluated on mathematical reasoning and adversarial red teaming datasets, where it was benchmarked against more sophisticated monitors based on sequential hypothesis testing. The key finding is that this simple thresholding approach performs competitively with the more complex alternatives, suggesting that elaborate monitoring mechanisms may not be necessary for effective online safety monitoring.
items.citation: Mona Schirmer, Metod Jazbec, Alexander Timans et al.. Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs. arXiv. 2026

items.title: DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation
items.summary: This paper investigates a problem in on-policy self-distillation for training large language models to reason, where a single model serves as both teacher and student with different levels of information access. The authors identify two key failure modes of existing approaches: overfitting to in-domain patterns that suppresses exploration, and privileged information leakage, in which the student learns shortcuts tied to answer information unavailable at test time. To address these issues, they introduce DemoPSD, a framework that selectively applies teacher guidance by steering the student toward a reverse-KL barycenter target—a weighted geometric combination of the teacher and student distributions—where the blending at each token position is controlled by the measured discrepancy between the two distributions. The authors theoretically demonstrate that DemoPSD provably attenuates privileged information leakage and preserves exploration capacity under dense token-level distillation. Empirical evaluations across multiple scientific domains show that DemoPSD outperforms competing self-distillation baselines while maintaining higher training entropy and generalizing more robustly to out-of-distribution benchmarks.
items.citation: Yunhe Li, Hao Shi, Wenhao Liu et al.. DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents
items.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of building traffic simulation agents that both realistically imitate recorded driving behavior and can be steered along interpretable behavioral dimensions for testing autonomous systems. The authors developed Controllable Neural Variational Agents, a framework that infers per-agent behavioral latents from discounted returns using a closed-form variational update, and combines this with a rectified-flow trajectory generator trained with classifier-free guidance. To address sparse reward signals, they introduced soft eligibility gates that replace hard binary thresholds with smooth exponential decay to preserve gradient information for agents near threshold boundaries. Evaluated on a large-scale motion dataset, the framework achieves competitive realism while enabling per-channel controllability over speed, acceleration, safety, and map compliance that standard imitation-only models lack. The work also demonstrates that monotone controllability responses depend on the soft eligibility mechanism, and cautions that steering metrics must be interpreted alongside physical plausibility checks to avoid reward-hacking artifacts.
items.citation: Juanwu Lu, Junyu Zhu, Ziran Wang. Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents. arXiv. 2026

items.title: The Debris Disk Host $β$ Piscis Austrinus is a Rapidly Rotating Star Seen Nearly Pole-On
items.summary: The paper investigated β Piscis Austrinus, a debris disk host star whose unusual spectral line shapes had previously led researchers to suspect it was a rapidly rotating star viewed nearly pole-on. Using a spectral synthesis code applied to high-dispersion spectra, the authors modeled the star's inclination, photospheric properties, and rotation rate, with additional temperature constraints derived from iron line ratios. The analysis confirmed that the star is oriented nearly pole-on and rotates at close to its critical velocity, producing substantial gravity darkening such that the polar temperature greatly exceeds the equatorial temperature and the apparent luminosity significantly overestimates the true luminosity. The same technique was validated by recovering a known nearly pole-on orientation for Vega through spectroscopy alone, consistent with interferometric results. Comparison with stellar evolutionary models yielded a mass and an age for β PsA that is consistent with the age of its companion star estimated from lithium depletion, demonstrating that spectroscopy alone can reliably characterize rapidly rotating stars seen nearly pole-on.
items.citation: Colin Kane, Russel White, Jeremy Jones et al.. The Debris Disk Host $β$ Piscis Austrinus is a Rapidly Rotating Star Seen Nearly Pole-On. arXiv. 2026

items.title: Cryogenic RF characterization of the MAGO cavity for high-frequency gravitational-wave detection
items.summary: This paper investigated the use of superconducting radio-frequency cavities as resonant sensors for detecting gravitational waves in the kilohertz-to-megahertz frequency range. The researchers characterized a prototype superconducting niobium cavity with an unconventional geometry specifically designed to achieve narrow separation between electromagnetic modes, subjecting it to cryogenic testing at temperatures down to two kelvin. The cavity underwent adapted surface preparation procedures and mechanical tuning, which achieved the targeted narrow mode splitting at cryogenic temperatures along with high electromagnetic quality factors. The measurements also revealed phase transfer characteristics suitable for stable low-level radio-frequency control, indications of mode coupling potentially arising from one-point multipacting, and mechanical quality factors for the cavity's eigenmodes that fell significantly below commonly assumed theoretical values. Overall, the study demonstrates that established superconducting radio-frequency preparation and characterization techniques can be successfully applied to non-standard resonator geometries, providing critical experimental input for the design of future gravitational-wave detectors based on this technology.
items.citation: Can Dokuyucu, Bianca Giaccone, Tom Krokotsch et al.. Cryogenic RF characterization of the MAGO cavity for high-frequency gravitational-wave detection. arXiv. 2026

items.title: TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution
items.summary: This paper addresses a gap in benchmarks for evaluating automated test generation and update tools, specifically the lack of execution-grounded evaluation that ties tests to real code changes. The authors introduce TestEvo-Bench, a benchmark mined from open-source Java repositories that packages real commit histories with environment configurations to support metrics such as pass rate, code coverage, and mutation score across two tracks: generating new tests for changed code and updating existing failing tests to reflect new behavior. A key design feature is that the benchmark is "live," with timestamps attached to each task and an automated pipeline for periodic updates, allowing evaluations to be restricted to tasks postdating a model's training cutoff in order to mitigate data leakage. Experiments with several state-of-the-art agent and foundation model combinations showed strong overall performance on both tracks, but success rates dropped notably on the most recent tasks and under constrained per-task computational budgets. These findings suggest that current agents may benefit partly from training data overlap and struggle when operating under realistic resource constraints.
items.citation: Jiale Amber Wang, Kaiyuan Wang, Pengyu Nie. TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution. arXiv. 2026

items.title: AI-Enhanced Point-of-Care Diagnostics for Infectious Diseases in Resource-Limited Settings: A Scoping Review.
items.summary: This scoping review systematically mapped research on artificial intelligence-enhanced point-of-care and rapid diagnostic technologies for infectious diseases in resource-limited settings, examining disease coverage, geographic representation, and validation quality. Following established scoping review methodology, the authors searched five major databases for studies published over roughly a decade, ultimately including over two hundred studies after screening. The field was found to be growing rapidly, dominated by work on a narrow triad of diseases while neglected tropical diseases received far less attention, and convolutional neural networks were the most commonly applied AI approach across imaging and biosensor modalities. A critical finding was that prospective field validation was rare, with the vast majority of studies either not reporting validation level or relying on retrospective or laboratory-based testing rather than real-world deployment. The authors conclude that despite rapid growth, the field suffers from significant gaps in validation rigor, disease equity, and geographic coverage, and call for future work to prioritize field validation, broaden disease focus, and meaningfully involve end-user communities from the outset of study design.
items.venue: Tropical medicine & international health : TM & IH
items.citation: Farquhar H. AI-Enhanced Point-of-Care Diagnostics for Infectious Diseases in Resource-Limited Settings: A Scoping Review.. Tropical medicine & international health : TM & IH. 2026

items.title: Infectious disease diagnostic competencies of medical students as a fundamental basis for physicians' resilience in addressing future infectious disease challenges.
items.summary: This paper examines the role of infectious disease diagnostic competencies in medical education as a foundation for building resilient physicians capable of addressing future infectious disease challenges. The authors argue that structured, practice-oriented training in medical microbiology, virology, and hygiene is essential because these skills underpin rational antimicrobial therapy, outbreak control, and the interruption of infection chains. The paper emphasizes that competencies such as correct sample collection, appropriate test selection, and critical interpretation of microbiological findings cannot be adequately acquired through self-directed learning formats alone. The authors contend that these skills must be embedded within the broader framework of the One-Health concept, which recognizes the interconnectedness of human, animal, and ecosystem health. Ultimately, the paper makes the case that fostering infection-related competencies in medical students is indispensable for combating antimicrobial resistance and ensuring long-term professional resilience against infectious disease threats.
items.citation: Brandt C, Becker K, Ciesek S et al.. Infectious disease diagnostic competencies of medical students as a fundamental basis for physicians' resilience in addressing future infectious disease challenges.. GMS infectious diseases. 2026

items.title: From Control to Eradication: The Role of Point-of-Care Testing in Modernising Australia's Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) Disease Management.
items.summary: This review examines the current state of bovine viral diarrhoea virus management in Australia and evaluates how diagnostic innovation could transform national disease control efforts. The authors analyse the limitations of Australia's decentralised, non-compulsory biosecurity framework, which places responsibility on individual producers and has resulted in a fragmented approach that has allowed the disease to remain endemic across beef and dairy systems. The review focuses on persistently infected animals as the primary transmission reservoir and argues that their rapid identification and culling is central to any viable eradication strategy. The authors propose that field-deployable point-of-care diagnostics, including lateral flow assays, nucleic acid amplification tests, and biosensors, represent a transformative tool capable of enabling real-time crush-side diagnosis and high-throughput surveillance at scale. They conclude that integrating these technologies with coordinated biosecurity measures and vaccination strategies would provide a scientifically sound and commercially viable pathway toward systematic national eradication of the virus.
items.citation: Ogada S, Naseem MN, Ranjbar S et al.. From Control to Eradication: The Role of Point-of-Care Testing in Modernising Australia's Bovine Viral Diarrhoea (BVD) Disease Management.. Viruses. 2026

items.title: Establishing an EU-compliant diagnostic facility for infectious diseases under war conditions in Poltava, Ukraine.
items.summary: This paper describes the establishment of advanced infectious disease diagnostic capabilities in a Ukrainian regional hospital during active wartime conditions. The authors implemented EU-compliant serological testing, automated PCR, and bacterial identification with antibiotic susceptibility testing at a hospital in Poltava, a region under heightened infectious disease burden due to the Russian invasion, mass displacement, and prior reliance on low-sensitivity rapid tests. The initiative was structured around a German-Ukrainian hospital partnership, featuring participatory equipment selection, hands-on staff training conducted in Germany, jointly developed operating procedures, and adaptive supply chain management. Over roughly fifteen months of operation, the laboratories conducted tens of thousands of tests and identified previously unrecognized cases of HIV, hepatitis B and C, Lyme disease, toxoplasmosis, and antibiotic-resistant bacterial infections. The authors conclude that high-quality diagnostic infrastructure can be successfully built in conflict zones when partnership design emphasizes practical simplicity, shared decision-making, and contingency planning, and they propose this model as a template for replication across other Ukrainian regions.
items.citation: Zelinskyy G, Koval T, Schrammel U et al.. Establishing an EU-compliant diagnostic facility for infectious diseases under war conditions in Poltava, Ukraine.. Frontiers in public health. 2026

items.title: Navigating the Future of Respiratory Infections: Key Insights From International Congress in Singapore, 17-20 September 2025.
items.summary: This paper summarizes the major scientific themes and discussions from an international conference focused on respiratory infections and respiratory viruses. The authors reviewed plenary sessions, symposia, and panel discussions, organizing findings into domains covering therapeutics, host response, vaccination, surveillance, diagnostics, and research infrastructure. Key themes included the development of long-acting and broadly active antivirals, combination therapy strategies, and a notable conceptual shift away from purely pathogen-centred management toward frameworks that incorporate the host inflammatory response as a central contributor to disease severity. Advances in vaccines targeting conserved viral components, adaptive platform trials, and integrated surveillance using genomic sequencing and metagenomics within a One Health framework were also highlighted. The authors conclude that clinical care, translational science, and public health preparedness are becoming increasingly interconnected, and that sustained investment across these areas is essential for both routine care and pandemic readiness.
items.citation: Morvil N, Goh WGW, Zheng C et al.. Navigating the Future of Respiratory Infections: Key Insights From International Congress in Singapore, 17-20 September 2025.. Influenza and other respiratory viruses. 2026

items.title: Biomedical technologies in context: Acceptability of a screening tool for sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in Zimbabwe and South Africa.
items.summary: This study examined the prospective acceptability of a novel point-of-care screening tool called the genital inflammation test, designed to detect genital inflammation as a proxy indicator for sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in asymptomatic women. Researchers conducted in-depth interviews and focus group discussions with women, healthcare professionals, and decision-makers in Zimbabwe and South Africa, analyzing responses through an established framework of acceptability. Women expressed strong interest in tools supporting sexual health and viewed diagnostic testing positively, but their enthusiasm diminished when they learned the test would require follow-up confirmatory diagnostics, due to concerns about cost and limited availability of those tests. Healthcare professionals and decision-makers similarly questioned the tool's practical utility within resource-constrained health systems, while acknowledging its potential to catalyze demand for more affordable and infrastructure-light diagnostics. The findings highlight that acceptability of biomedical technologies is fundamentally shaped by health system context, and that successful implementation would require clear communication strategies, affordable confirmatory testing, and co-designed care pathways.
items.citation: Karumazondo J, Bernays S, Naidoo J et al.. Biomedical technologies in context: Acceptability of a screening tool for sexually transmitted infections and bacterial vaginosis in Zimbabwe and South Africa.. Global public health. 2026

items.title: The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria
items.summary: This paper presents an updated international clinical guideline for urticaria, a mast cell-driven condition characterized by the rapid appearance of wheals, angioedema, or both. The guideline was developed using Cochrane methodology and the GRADE framework, with a large consensus process involving delegates from numerous national and international societies across many countries. It covers the definition and classification of urticaria, distinguishing between acute urticaria and chronic forms — the latter subdivided into chronic spontaneous and chronic inducible urticaria. The guideline provides evidence-based and expert-guided recommendations for diagnosis and management across the different subtypes, acknowledging that chronic urticaria significantly impairs quality of life and functioning while noting that novel therapies are now available. The resulting recommendations were formally acknowledged and accepted by the European Union of Medical Specialists.
items.citation: T. Zuberbier, Z. Abdul Hameed Ansari, A. H. Abdul Latiff et al.. The International Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis and Management of Urticaria. Allergy. 2026

items.title: Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease
items.summary: This review examines the role of lipid metabolism in maintaining cellular homeostasis and its dysregulation across a range of diseases. The authors synthesize the research history of lipid metabolism with a particular focus on how immune cells — including macrophages, T cells, B cells, and neutrophils — depend on lipid metabolites for proliferation, differentiation, and activation in both innate and adaptive immunity. The review covers lipid transport, signaling pathways, and multilevel regulatory mechanisms spanning genetic, epigenetic, posttranscriptional, and posttranslational processes. Dysregulated lipid metabolism is discussed in the context of autoimmune diseases, cancer, neurodegeneration, cardiovascular disease, aging, and metabolic disorders. The authors conclude by surveying therapeutic strategies that target lipid metabolism, ongoing clinical trial progress, and emerging directions such as lipid-derived biomarkers and novel treatment approaches.
items.citation: Zhili Li, Wende Deng, Lanxuan Yang et al.. Lipid metabolism in homeostasis and disease. Signal Transduction and Targeted Therapy. 2026

items.title: Digital twins in oncology: From predictive modelling to personalised treatment strategies
items.summary: This narrative review examined the current state and potential of digital twin technology applied across oncology, from diagnosis through survivorship care. The authors conducted a comprehensive literature search across multiple major databases, including grey literature and conference proceedings, to synthesise evidence on how digital twins are being developed, validated, and used in cancer settings. They found that digital twins demonstrate utility across a broad range of oncological applications, including precision treatment selection, radiotherapy optimisation, drug development, immunotherapy modelling, and surgical planning, with artificial intelligence and multi-omics data integration enabling dynamic simulation of tumour behaviour and treatment response. Despite this promise, the review identified persistent challenges in data integration, computational scalability, model validation, and ethical governance that currently hinder clinical translation. The authors conclude that realising the full potential of digital twin technology in precision oncology will require interdisciplinary collaboration and the development of appropriate regulatory frameworks.
items.citation: David B. Olawade, Emmanuel O. Oisakede, Oluwakemi Jumoke Bello et al.. Digital twins in oncology: From predictive modelling to personalised treatment strategies. Critical Reviews in Oncology/Hematology. 2026

items.title: Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming
items.summary: This review examines the gut microbiome as a causal driver of colorectal cancer, synthesizing molecular, ecological, and translational evidence across immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes. The authors describe how dysbiosis characterized by enrichment of pathobionts such as Fusobacterium nucleatum, colibactin-producing Escherichia coli, and enterotoxigenic Bacteroides fragilis, alongside depletion of short-chain-fatty-acid-producing commensals, promotes carcinogenesis through DNA damage, epithelial barrier disruption, and chronic inflammation. Specific bacterial genotoxins and metabolites, including colibactin, cytolethal distending toxin, and indolimines, are shown to leave defined mutational signatures directly traceable in human cancer genomes. The review conceptualizes colorectal cancer as a biofilm-structured, polymicrobial ecosystem disease in which microbial consortia cooperate to sustain immune evasion and tumor growth within the tumor microenvironment. Building on this framework, the authors highlight microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies—including dietary modulation, bacteriophage therapy, engineered biotherapeutics, and fecal microbiota transplantation—as translational avenues for precision prevention and treatment.
items.citation: Jhommara Bautista, Mónica Lamas‐Maceiras, Camila Hidalgo-Tinoco et al.. Gut microbiome–driven colorectal cancer via immune, metabolic, neural, and endocrine axes reprogramming. npj Biofilms and Microbiomes. 2026

items.title: EULAR recommendations for the management of rheumatoid arthritis with synthetic and biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs: 2025 update
items.summary: This paper presents the updated clinical guidance from a major European rheumatology alliance for managing rheumatoid arthritis using synthetic and biologic disease-modifying antirheumatic drugs.
